packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ANTEROMEDIAL PARAPATELLAR APPROACH
packs.summary: This approach can be used to access the medial tibial plateau by elevating the sartorial fascia and pes anserinus while pro- tecting the superficial medial collateral ligament insertion on the tibia. Access through this approach allows improved exposure of medial articular surface comminution. *FIGURE 1.42 Method of mobilizing and retracting common peroneal nerve when approaching proximal fibula posterolaterally. A, Anatomic relationships. B, Part of peroneus longus that arises from lateral surface of fibular head proximal to common peroneal nerve has been detached, allowing nerve to be retracted over fibular head. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.41.* *FIGURE 1.43 Anteromedial approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.42.*
packs.text:
This approach can be used to access the medial tibial plateau by elevating the sartorial fascia and pes anserinus while pro-
tecting the superficial medial collateral ligament insertion on the tibia. Access through this approach allows improved
exposure of medial articular surface comminution.

*FIGURE 1.42 Method of mobilizing and retracting common peroneal nerve when approaching proximal fibula posterolaterally. A, Anatomic relationships. B, Part of peroneus longus that arises from lateral surface of fibular head proximal to common peroneal nerve has been detached, allowing nerve to be retracted over fibular head. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.41.*

*FIGURE 1.43 Anteromedial approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.42.*

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (VON LANGENBECK)
packs.summary: 1. Begin the incision at the medial border of the quadri- ceps tendon 7 to 10 cm proximal to the patella, curve it around the medial border of the patella and back to- ward the midline, and end it at or distal to the tibial tuberosity. As a more cosmetically pleasing alternative, a longitudinal incision centered over the patella can be made, reflecting the subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia over the patella medially by blunt dissection to the medial border of the patella. 2. Divide and retract the fascia. 3. Deepen the dissection between the vastus medialis muscle and the medial border of the quadriceps tendon and incise the capsule and synovium along this medial border and the medial border of the patella and patellar tendon. 4. Retract the patella laterally and flex the knee...
packs.text:
1. Begin the incision at the medial border of the quadri-
ceps tendon 7 to 10 cm proximal to the patella, curve it
around the medial border of the patella and back to-
ward the midline, and end it at or distal to the tibial
tuberosity. As a more cosmetically pleasing alternative,
a longitudinal incision centered over the patella can be
made, reflecting the subcutaneous tissue and superficial
fascia over the patella medially by blunt dissection to
the medial border of the patella.

2. Divide and retract the fascia.

3. Deepen the dissection between the vastus medialis
muscle and the medial border of the quadriceps tendon
and incise the capsule and synovium along this medial
border and the medial border of the patella and patellar
tendon.

4. Retract the patella laterally and flex the knee to gain an unobstructed view of the anterior compartment of the joint and the suprapatellar bursa. Divide the ligamen-
tum mucosa if necessary.

5. Attain more comprehensive access to the joint in the
following ways: (1) extending the incision proximally,
(2) extending the proximal part of the incision obliquely
medially and separating the fibers of the vastus media-
lis, (3) dividing the medial alar fold and adjacent fat pad

longitudinally, and (4) mobilizing the medial part of the
insertion of the patellar tendon subperiosteally.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — CAMPBELL'S CONCEPTS
packs.summary: Precautions and Recommendations in Anteromedial Parapatellar Approach When any anteromedial approach is made, including one for meniscectomy, find and protect the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve. No single incision on the anteromedial aspect of the knee can avoid it for sure because several variations exist in the location of the infrapatellar branch (Fig. 1.44). If contracture of the quadriceps prevents sufficient ex- posure, detach or osteotomize the tibial tuberosity, and reattach it later with a screw or, alternatively, with three lag screws engaging the posterior tibial cortex as described by Fernandez. This technique achieves rigid fixation and allows early postoperative rehabilitation. If the parapatellar approach is used for total knee arthroplasty (nonresurfaced...
packs.text:
Precautions and Recommendations in
Anteromedial Parapatellar Approach

When any anteromedial approach is made, including one for meniscectomy, find and protect the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve. No single incision on the anteromedial aspect of the knee can avoid it for sure because several variations exist in the location of the infrapatellar branch (Fig. 1.44).

If contracture of the quadriceps prevents sufficient ex-
posure, detach or osteotomize the tibial tuberosity, and
reattach it later with a screw or, alternatively, with
three lag screws engaging the posterior tibial cortex as
described by Fernandez. This technique achieves rigid
fixation and allows early postoperative rehabilitation.

If the parapatellar approach is used for total knee arthroplasty (nonresurfaced patella), repair of the medial patellofemoral ligament is recommended at closure to prevent significant medial capsular dehiscence and resultant loading of the lateral patellar facet and increased anterior knee pain (Keshmiri et al.).

*FIGURE 1.44 Saphenous nerve runs posterior to sartorius muscle, piercing fascia lata between tendons of sartorius and gracilis muscles and becoming subcutaneous on medial aspect of leg. On medial aspect of knee, it gives off large infrapatellar branch, location and distribution of which is variable, to supply skin over anteromedial aspect of knee.*

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — SUBVASTUS (SOUTHERN)
packs.summary: ANTEROMEDIAL APPROACH TO THE KNEE Problems with patellar dislocation, subluxation, and osteo- necrosis after total knee arthroplasty performed through an anteromedial parapatellar approach led to the rediscov- ery of the subvastus or Southern anteromedial approach first described by Erkes in 1929. This approach preserves the vascularity of the patella by sparing the intramuscular articular branch of the descending genicular artery and pre- serves the quadriceps tendon, supplying more stability to the patellofemoral joint in total knee arthroplasty. NOTE! This approach cannot be used in patients with a thigh girth of approximately 55 cm, because the patella cannot be everted during total knee arthroplasty. The approach is useful for lesser anteromedial and medial knee procedures. The...
packs.text:
ANTEROMEDIAL APPROACH TO
THE KNEE

Problems with patellar dislocation, subluxation, and osteo-
necrosis after total knee arthroplasty performed through
an anteromedial parapatellar approach led to the rediscov-
ery of the subvastus or Southern anteromedial approach
first described by Erkes in 1929. This approach preserves
the vascularity of the patella by sparing the intramuscular
articular branch of the descending genicular artery and pre-
serves the quadriceps tendon, supplying more stability to
the patellofemoral joint in total knee arthroplasty. NOTE!
This approach cannot be used in patients with a thigh
girth of approximately 55 cm, because the patella cannot
be everted during total knee arthroplasty. The approach is
useful for lesser anteromedial and medial knee procedures.
The relative contraindications are:

▪ Earlier major knee arthroplasty
▪ Patient weight > 200 lb
▪ Thigh girth > 55 cm in patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (Erkes, as described by Hofmann, Plaster, and Murdock)
packs.summary: 1. Exsanguinate the limb and inflate the tourniquet with the knee flexed to at least 90 degrees to prevent tenodesis of the extensor mechanism. 2. Make a straight anterior skin incision, beginning 8 cm above the patella, carrying it distally just medial and 2 cm distal to the tibial tubercle. 3. Incise the superficial fascia slightly medial to the patella (Fig. 1.45A) and bluntly dissect it off the vastus medialis muscle fascia down to the muscle insertion (Fig. 1.45B). 4. Find the inferior edge of the vastus medialis and bluntly dissect it off the periosteum and intermuscular septum for 10 cm proximal to the adductor tubercle. 5. Find the tendinous insertion of the muscle on the me- dial patellar retinaculum (Fig. 1.45C), lift the vastus medialis muscle anteriorly, and perform an...
packs.text:
1. Exsanguinate the limb and inflate the tourniquet with the knee flexed to at least 90 degrees to prevent tenodesis of the extensor mechanism.
2. Make a straight anterior skin incision, beginning 8 cm above the patella, carrying it distally just medial and 2 cm distal to the tibial tubercle.

3. Incise the superficial fascia slightly medial to the patella (Fig. 1.45A) and bluntly dissect it off the vastus medialis muscle fascia down to the muscle insertion (Fig. 1.45B).
4. Find the inferior edge of the vastus medialis and bluntly dissect it off the periosteum and intermuscular septum for 10 cm proximal to the adductor tubercle.
5. Find the tendinous insertion of the muscle on the me-
dial patellar retinaculum (Fig. 1.45C), lift the vastus
medialis muscle anteriorly, and perform an L-shaped
arthrotomy beginning medially through the vastus in-
sertion on the medial patellar retinaculum and carrying
it along the medial edge of the patella.
6. Partially release the medial edge of the patellar tendon
and evert the patella laterally with the knee extended
(Fig. 1.45D).

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ANTEROLATERAL APPROACH TO THE KNEE
packs.summary: Usually, the anterolateral approach is not as satisfactory as the anteromedial one for arthroplasty, primarily because it is more difficult to displace the patella medially than later- ally. It also requires a longer incision, and often the patellar tendon must be partially freed subperiosteally or subcorti- cally. However, the approach allows extension into a lateral approach to the femur for intraarticular fractures where joint access is needed.
packs.text:
Usually, the anterolateral approach is not as satisfactory as the anteromedial one for arthroplasty, primarily because it is more difficult to displace the patella medially than later-
ally. It also requires a longer incision, and often the patellar tendon must be partially freed subperiosteally or subcorti-
cally. However, the approach allows extension into a lateral approach to the femur for intraarticular fractures where
joint access is needed.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (KOCHER)
packs.summary: 1. Begin the incision 7.5 cm proximal to the patella at the insertion of the vastus lateralis muscle into the quadri- ceps tendon; continue it distally along the lateral border of this tendon, the patella, and the patellar tendon; and end it 2.5 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity. 2. Deepen the dissection through the joint capsule. 3. Retract the patella medially, with the tendons attached to it, and expose the articular surface of the joint. Avoid injury to the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus.
packs.text:
1. Begin the incision 7.5 cm proximal to the patella at the insertion of the vastus lateralis muscle into the quadri-
ceps tendon; continue it distally along the lateral border
of this tendon, the patella, and the patellar tendon; and
end it 2.5 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity.
2. Deepen the dissection through the joint capsule.
3. Retract the patella medially, with the tendons attached
to it, and expose the articular surface of the joint. Avoid
injury to the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ■ Approach for total knee arthroplasty in fixed valgus knees
packs.summary: Satish et al. found the modified Keblish approach useful in total knee arthroplasty in patients with fixed valgus knees. The approach relies on a quadriceps snip and coronal Z-plasty of the lateral retinacular capsule complex. The lateral retinacular complex is separated into two layers: deep (cap- sule and synovium) and superficial. The lateral parapatellar arthrotomy is performed 3 to 7 cm lateral to the patella, and the deep and superficial layers are separated with dissection carried medially toward the patella. The superficial layer is kept attached to the patella, and the deep layer stays attached to the iliotibial band. At closure, the layers are approximated in an expanded fashion (Fig. 1.47). *FIGURE 1.45 Subvastus anteromedial approach. A, Superficial fascia is incised medial...
packs.text:
Satish et al. found the modified Keblish approach useful in
total knee arthroplasty in patients with fixed valgus knees.
The approach relies on a quadriceps snip and coronal
Z-plasty of the lateral retinacular capsule complex. The lateral
retinacular complex is separated into two layers: deep (cap-
sule and synovium) and superficial. The lateral parapatellar
arthrotomy is performed 3 to 7 cm lateral to the patella, and
the deep and superficial layers are separated with dissection
carried medially toward the patella. The superficial layer is
kept attached to the patella, and the deep layer stays attached
to the iliotibial band. At closure, the layers are approximated
in an expanded fashion (Fig. 1.47).

*FIGURE 1.45 Subvastus anteromedial approach. A, Superficial fascia is incised medial to patella. B, Superficial fascia is bluntly elevated from perimuscular fascia of vastus medialis down to its insertion on medial patellar retinaculum. C, Tendinous insertion elevated by blunt dissection.
Dashed line indicates arthrotomy. D, Patella is everted, and knee is extended. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.43.*

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (HENDERSON)
packs.summary: 1. With the knee flexed between 60 and 90 degrees, make a curved incision on the lateral side of the knee, just anterior to the biceps femoris tendon and the head of the fibula, and avoid the common peroneal nerve, which passes over the lateral aspect of the neck of the fibula. 2. In the proximal part of the incision, trace the anterior surface of the lateral intermuscular septum to the linea aspera 5 cm proximal to the lateral femoral condyle. 3. Expose the lateral femoral condyle and the origin of the fibular collateral ligament.* 4. The tendon of the popliteus muscle lies between the biceps tendon and the fibular collateral ligament; mobilize and retract it posteriorly and expose the posterolateral aspect of the joint capsule. 5. Make a longitudinal incision through the capsule and...
packs.text:
1. With the knee flexed between 60 and 90 degrees, make
a curved incision on the lateral side of the knee, just
anterior to the biceps femoris tendon and the head of
the fibula, and avoid the common peroneal nerve, which
passes over the lateral aspect of the neck of the fibula.

2. In the proximal part of the incision, trace the anterior surface of the lateral intermuscular septum to the linea aspera 5 cm proximal to the lateral femoral condyle.

3. Expose the lateral femoral condyle and the origin of the fibular collateral ligament.*

4. The tendon of the popliteus muscle lies between the biceps tendon and the fibular collateral ligament; mobilize and retract it posteriorly and expose the posterolateral aspect of the joint capsule.

5. Make a longitudinal incision through the capsule and synovium of the posterior compartment. To see the in-
sertion of the muscle fibers of the short head of the bi-
ceps muscle onto the long head of the biceps, develop
the interval between the lateral head of the quadriceps
muscle and the long head of the biceps tendon. To iso-
late the common peroneal nerve, dissect directly poste-
rior to the long head of the biceps. These intervals are
helpful in the repair of the posterolateral corner of the
knee.

*FIGURE 1.46 A–C, Kocher anterolateral approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.44.*

(Redrawn from Satish BRJ, Ganesan JC, Chandran P, et al.: Efficacy and midterm results of lateral parapatellar approach without tibial tubercle osteotomy for primary total knee arthroplasty in fixed valgus knees, J Arthroplasty 28:1751, 2013.)*

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (HENDERSON)
packs.summary: 1. With the knee flexed 90 degrees, make a curved inci- sion, slightly convex anteriorly and approximately 7.5 cm long, distally from the adductor tubercle and along the course of the tibial collateral ligament, anterior to the relaxed tendons of the semimembranosus, semitendino- sus, sartorius, and gracilis muscles. 2. Expose and incise the oblique part of the tibial collateral ligament and incise the capsule longitudinally and enter the posteromedial compartment of the knee posterior to the tibial collateral ligament, retracting the hamstring tendons posteriorly. *FIGURE 1.48 Henderson posterolateral approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.45.*
packs.text:
1. With the knee flexed 90 degrees, make a curved inci-
sion, slightly convex anteriorly and approximately 7.5 cm
long, distally from the adductor tubercle and along the
course of the tibial collateral ligament, anterior to the
relaxed tendons of the semimembranosus, semitendino-
sus, sartorius, and gracilis muscles.

2. Expose and incise the oblique part of the tibial collateral ligament and incise the capsule longitudinally and enter the posteromedial compartment of the knee posterior to the tibial collateral ligament, retracting the hamstring tendons posteriorly.

*FIGURE 1.48 Henderson posterolateral approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.45.*

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — CAMPBELL'S CONCEPTS
packs.summary: Considering the Median Septum in Posterolateral and Posteromedial Approaches to the Knee A median septum separating the posterior aspect of the knee into two compartments is present in some patients. The posterior cruciate ligament is extrasynovial and projects anteriorly in the septum, contributing to the partition between the two posterior compartments. The middle genicular artery courses anteriorly in the septum to nourish the tissues of the intercondylar notch of the femur (Fig. 1.50). The median septum assumes significant importance when exploring the posterior aspect of the knee for a loose body or when posterior draining of the joint is necessary for pyogenic arthritis. In the latter, remember to drain both posterior compartments.
packs.text:
Considering the Median Septum in Posterolateral and Posteromedial Approaches to the Knee

A median septum separating the posterior aspect of the knee into two compartments is present in some patients. The posterior cruciate ligament is extrasynovial and projects anteriorly in the septum, contributing to the partition between the two posterior compartments. The middle genicular artery courses anteriorly in the septum to nourish the tissues of the intercondylar notch of the femur (Fig. 1.50).

The median septum assumes significant importance when exploring the posterior aspect of the knee for a loose body or when posterior draining of the joint is necessary for pyogenic arthritis. In the latter, remember to drain both posterior compartments.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — MEDIAL APPROACHES TO THE KNEE AND SUPPORTING STRUCTURES
packs.summary: Usually, the entire medial meniscus can be excised through a medial parapatellar incision about 5 cm long. If the posterior horn of the meniscus cannot be excised through this incision, a separate posteromedial Henderson approach can be made (see Fig. 1.49). The anterior and posterior compartments may be entered, however, through an approach in which only one incision is made through the skin, but two incisions are used through the deeper structures; this type of approach is rarely necessary.
packs.text:
Usually, the entire medial meniscus can be excised through a
medial parapatellar incision about 5 cm long. If the posterior
horn of the meniscus cannot be excised through this incision,
a separate posteromedial Henderson approach can be made
(see Fig. 1.49). The anterior and posterior compartments
may be entered, however, through an approach in which only
one incision is made through the skin, but two incisions are
used through the deeper structures; this type of approach is
rarely necessary.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — MEDIAL APPROACH TO THE KNEE
packs.summary: The Cave approach is a curved incision that allows exposure of the anterior and posterior compartments. *FIGURE 1.49 A–C, Henderson posteromedial approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.46.*
packs.text:
The Cave approach is a curved incision that allows exposure
of the anterior and posterior compartments.

*FIGURE 1.49 A–C, Henderson posteromedial approach to knee joint. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.46.*

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (CAVE)
packs.summary: 1. With the knee flexed at a right angle, find the medial femoral epicondyle and begin the incision 1 cm posterior to and on a level with it approximately 1 cm proximal to the joint line. Carry the incision distally and anteriorly to a point 0.5 cm distal to the joint line and anterior to the border of the patellar tendon. 2. After reflecting the subcutaneous tissues, expose the anterior compartment through an incision that begins anterior to the tibial collateral ligament, continues dis- tally and anteriorly in a curve like that of the skin inci- sion, and ends just distal to the joint line (Fig. 1.51A). 3. To expose the posterior compartment, make a second deep incision posterior to the tibial collateral ligament, from the level of the femoral epicondyle straight distally across the...
packs.text:
1. With the knee flexed at a right angle, find the medial femoral epicondyle and begin the incision 1 cm posterior to and on a level with it approximately 1 cm proximal to the joint line. Carry the incision distally and anteriorly to a point 0.5 cm distal to the joint line and anterior to the border of the patellar tendon.

2. After reflecting the subcutaneous tissues, expose the anterior compartment through an incision that begins anterior to the tibial collateral ligament, continues dis-
tally and anteriorly in a curve like that of the skin inci-
sion, and ends just distal to the joint line (Fig. 1.51A).

3. To expose the posterior compartment, make a second
deep incision posterior to the tibial collateral ligament,

from the level of the femoral epicondyle straight distally
across the joint line (Fig. 1.51B).

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (HOPPENFELD AND DEBOER)
packs.summary: 1. With the patient supine and the affected knee flexed about 60 degrees, place the foot on the opposite shin and abduct and externally rotate the hip. 2. Begin the incision 2 cm proximal to the adductor tu- bercle of the femur, curve it anteroinferior about 3 cm medial to the medial border of the patella, and end it 6 cm distal to the joint line on the anteromedial aspect of the tibia (Fig. 1.52A). 3. Retract the skin flaps to expose the fascia of the knee and extend the exposure from the midline anteriorly to the posteromedial corner of the knee (Fig. 1.52B). 4. Cut the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve and bury its end in fat; preserve the saphenous nerve itself and the long saphenous vein. *FIGURE 1.50 Median septum separating two posterior compartments of knee. Note...
packs.text:
1. With the patient supine and the affected knee flexed about 60 degrees, place the foot on the opposite shin and abduct and externally rotate the hip.

2. Begin the incision 2 cm proximal to the adductor tu-
bercle of the femur, curve it anteroinferior about 3 cm
medial to the medial border of the patella, and end it 6
cm distal to the joint line on the anteromedial aspect of
the tibia (Fig. 1.52A).

3. Retract the skin flaps to expose the fascia of the knee
and extend the exposure from the midline anteriorly to
the posteromedial corner of the knee (Fig. 1.52B).

4. Cut the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve and bury its end in fat; preserve the saphenous nerve itself and the long saphenous vein.

*FIGURE 1.50 Median septum separating two posterior compartments of knee. Note fenestra at proximal pole. Synovial septum invests cruciate ligaments and contains branch of middle genicular artery.*

5. Longitudinally incise the fascia along the anterior border
of the sartorius, starting at the tibial attachment of the
muscle and extending it to 5 cm proximal to the joint line.

6. Flex the knee further and allow the sartorius to retract
posteriorly, exposing the semitendinosus and gracilis
muscles (Fig. 1.52C).

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (HOPPENFELD AND DEBOER)
packs.summary: 7. Retract all three components of the pes anserinus posteriory and expose the tibial attachment of the tibial collateral ligament, which inserts 6 to 7 cm distal to the joint line (Fig. 1.52D). 8. To open the joint anteriorly, make a longitudinal me- dial parapatellar incision through the retinaculum and synovium (Fig. 1.52E). 9. To expose the posterior third of the medial meniscus and the posteromedial corner of the knee, retract the three components of the pes anserinus posteriorly (Fig. 1.52F) and separate the medial head of the gas- trocnemius muscle from the posterior capsule of the knee to the midline by blunt dissection (Fig. 1.52G). 10. To open the joint posteriorly, make an incision through the capsule posterior to the tibial collateral ligament.
packs.text:
7. Retract all three components of the pes anserinus posteriory and expose the tibial attachment of the tibial collateral ligament, which inserts 6 to 7 cm distal to the joint line (Fig. 1.52D).

8. To open the joint anteriorly, make a longitudinal me-
dial parapatellar incision through the retinaculum and
synovium (Fig. 1.52E).

9. To expose the posterior third of the medial meniscus
and the posteromedial corner of the knee, retract the
three components of the pes anserinus posteriorly
(Fig. 1.52F) and separate the medial head of the gas-
trocnemius muscle from the posterior capsule of the
knee to the midline by blunt dissection (Fig. 1.52G).

10. To open the joint posteriorly, make an incision through
the capsule posterior to the tibial collateral ligament.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TRANSVERSE APPROACH TO
packs.summary: THE MENISCUS Using a transverse approach to the medial meniscus has the advantage that the scar has no contact with the femoral articular surface.
packs.text:
THE MENISCUS

Using a transverse approach to the medial meniscus has the
advantage that the scar has no contact with the femoral
articular surface.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 1.49
packs.summary: 1. Make a transverse incision 5 cm long at the level of the articular surface of the tibia, extending laterally *FIGURE 1.51 Exposure of anterior and posterior compartments of knee joint through one skin incision, according to Cave. A, Single skin incision. B, Two incisions through deep structures. C, Removal of meniscus. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.47.* *A* *B* *C* *D* *E* FIGURE 1.52 Medial approach to knee and supporting structures. cus is approached through transverse incisions in skin and cap-

from the medial border of the patellar tendon to
the anterior border of the tibial collateral ligament
(Fig. 1.53).

2. Incise the capsule along the same line and dissect the proximal edge of the divided capsule from the underly-
ing synovium and retract it proximally.

3. Open the synovium along the proximal border of the
medial meniscus. Charnley recommended making a
preliminary 1.5-cm opening into the small synovial sac
beneath the meniscus, introducing a blunt hook into it,

and turning the hook so that its end rests on the proxi-
mal surface of the meniscus. By cutting down on the
point of the hook, one can make the synovial incision at
the most distal level.

4. Divide the anterior attachment of the meniscus, retract the tibial collateral ligament, and complete the excision of the meniscus in the usual way (see Chapter 50).

5. When closing the incision, place the first suture in the synovium at the medial side near the collateral ligament while the knee is still flexed; if the joint is extended before the first suture is inserted, the posterior part of the synovial incision retracts under the tibial collateral ligament. To complete the suture line, extend the joint.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — Figure 1.53
packs.summary: 6. The transverse incision is not satisfactory for removing the lateral meniscus because it would require partial division of the iliotibial band. To avoid this, make an oblique incision 7.5 cm long centered over the joint line (see Fig. 1.53). 7. In the capsule, make a hockey-stick incision that runs transversely along the joint line and curves obliquely proximally along the anterior border of the iliotibial band for a short distance. 8. Undermine and retract the capsule and incise the syno- vial membrane transversely as previously described.

6. The transverse incision is not satisfactory for removing the lateral meniscus because it would require partial division of the iliotibial band. To avoid this, make an oblique incision 7.5 cm long centered over the joint line
(see Fig. 1.53).

7. In the capsule, make a hockey-stick incision that runs
transversely along the joint line and curves obliquely
proximally along the anterior border of the iliotibial
band for a short distance.

8. Undermine and retract the capsule and incise the syno-
vial membrane transversely as previously described.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (BRUSER)
packs.summary: 1. Place the patient supine and drape the limb to allow full flexion of the knee. Flex the knee fully so that the foot rests flat on the operating table. 2. Begin the incision anteriorly where the patellar tendon crosses the lateral joint line, continue it posteriorly along the joint line, and end it at an imaginary line extending from the proximal end of the fibula to the lateral femo- ral condyle (Fig. 1.54A). 3. Incise the subcutaneous tissue and expose the iliotibial band, the fibers of which are parallel with the skin incision when the knee is fully flexed (Fig. 1.54B). Split the band in line with its fibers. Posteriorly, take care to avoid injuring the relaxed fibular collateral ligament; it is protected by areolar tissue, which separates it from the iliotibial band. 4. Retract...
packs.text:
1. Place the patient supine and drape the limb to allow full flexion of the knee. Flex the knee fully so that the foot rests flat on the operating table.

2. Begin the incision anteriorly where the patellar tendon crosses the lateral joint line, continue it posteriorly along the joint line, and end it at an imaginary line extending from the proximal end of the fibula to the lateral femo-
ral condyle (Fig. 1.54A).

3. Incise the subcutaneous tissue and expose the iliotibial
band, the fibers of which are parallel with the skin incision
when the knee is fully flexed (Fig. 1.54B). Split the band
in line with its fibers. Posteriorly, take care to avoid injuring
the relaxed fibular collateral ligament; it is protected by
areolar tissue, which separates it from the iliotibial band.

4. Retract the margins of the iliotibial band; this is possible to achieve without much force because the band is relaxed when the knee and hip are flexed.

5. Locate the lateral inferior genicular artery, which lies outside the synovium between the collateral ligament and the posterolateral aspect of the meniscus.

6. Incise the synovium. The lateral meniscus lies in the depth
of the incision and can be excised completely (Fig. 1.54C).

7. With the knee flexed 90 degrees, close the synovium
(Fig. 1.54D); and with the knee extended, close the
deep fascia.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — LATERAL APPROACH TO THE KNEE
packs.summary: Brown et al. have developed an approach for lateral meniscectomy in which the knee is flexed to allow important structures to fall posteriorly as in the Bruser approach. In addi- tion, a varus strain is created to open the lateral joint space.
packs.text:
Brown et al. have developed an approach for lateral meniscectomy in which the knee is flexed to allow important structures to fall posteriorly as in the Bruser approach. In addi-
tion, a varus strain is created to open the lateral joint space.

A, Skin incision (see text). B, Broken line indicates proposed incision in iliotibial band, whose fibers, when knee is fully flexed, are parallel with skin incision. C, Knee has been extended slightly, and lateral meniscus is being excised. D, Lateral meniscus has been excised, and synovium is being closed. (Modified from Bruser DM: A direct lateral approach to the lateral compartment of the knee joint, J Bone Joint Surg 42B:348, 1960.) SEE TECHNIQUES 1.49 AND 1.50. *D* 2. Make a vertical, oblique, or transverse skin incision on the anterolateral aspect of the knee. 3. Find the anterior border of the iliotibial band and make an...
packs.text:
1. Place the patient supine with the extremity straight and with a small sandbag under the ipsilateral hip.

*A*

*B*

FIGURE 1.54 Bruser lateral approach to knee. A, Skin incision (see text). B, Broken line indicates proposed incision in iliotibial band, whose fibers, when knee is fully flexed, are parallel with skin incision. C, Knee has been extended slightly, and lateral meniscus is being excised. D, Lateral meniscus has been excised, and synovium is being closed. (Modified from Bruser DM: A direct lateral approach to the lateral compartment of the knee joint, J Bone Joint Surg 42B:348, 1960.) SEE TECHNIQUES 1.49 AND 1.50.

*D*

2. Make a vertical, oblique, or transverse skin incision on the anterolateral aspect of the knee.

3. Find the anterior border of the iliotibial band and make an incision in the fascia 0.5 to 1 cm anterior to the band in line with its fibers.

4. Incise the synovium in line with this incision and inspect the joint.

5. By sharp dissection, free the anterior horn of the menis-
cus.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (BROWN ET AL.)
packs.summary: 6. Flex the knee, cross the foot over the opposite knee, and push firmly toward the opposite hip, applying a varus force to the knee. Ensure the thigh on the in- volved side is in line with the sagittal plane of the trunk; the hip is flexed about 45 degrees and externally ro- tated about 40 degrees. Push, as described, until the joint space opens 3 to 5 mm. If necessary, internally rotate the tibia to bring the lateral tibial plateau into better view; however, this tends to close the joint space. 7. With proper retractors, expose the entire meniscus, which can be excised entirely by sharp dissection.
packs.text:
6. Flex the knee, cross the foot over the opposite knee,
and push firmly toward the opposite hip, applying a
varus force to the knee. Ensure the thigh on the in-
volved side is in line with the sagittal plane of the trunk;
the hip is flexed about 45 degrees and externally ro-
tated about 40 degrees. Push, as described, until the
joint space opens 3 to 5 mm. If necessary, internally
rotate the tibia to bring the lateral tibial plateau into
better view; however, this tends to close the joint space.

7. With proper retractors, expose the entire meniscus,
which can be excised entirely by sharp dissection.

packs.summary: 1. Place the patient supine with a sandbag beneath the ipsilateral buttock and flex the knee 90 degrees. 2. Begin the incision 3 cm lateral to the middle of the patella, extend it distally over Gerdy's tubercle on the tibia, and end it 4 to 5 cm distal to the joint line. Complete the incision proximally by curving it along the line of the femur (Fig. 1.55A). 3. Widely mobilize the skin flaps anteriorly and posteri- orly. 4. Incise the fascia between the iliotibial band and biceps femoris, carefully avoiding the common peroneal nerve on the posterior aspect of the biceps tendon (Fig. 1.55B). 5. Retract the iliotibial band anteriorly and the biceps fem- oris and common peroneal nerve posteriorly to expose *A* *C* *Lateral femoral condyle*

Complete the incision proximally by curving it along the line of the femur (Fig. 1.55A).

orly.

femoris, carefully avoiding the common peroneal

(Fig. 1.55B).

oris and common peroneal nerve posteriorly to expose

FIGURE 1.55 Lateral approach to knee and supporting structures. A, Skin incision. B, Incision
between biceps femoris and iliotibial band. C, Deep dissection (see text). (Modified from Hoppen-

Williams & Wilkins, 2003.) SEE TECHNIQUE 1.52.

the fibular collateral ligament and the posterolateral
corner of the knee capsule (Fig. 1.55C).

6. To expose the lateral meniscus, make a separate
lateral parapatellar incision through the fascia and
joint capsule (see Fig. 1.55B).

7. To avoid cutting the meniscus, begin the arthrotomy
2 cm proximal to the joint line.

8. To expose the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus,
find the origin of the lateral head of the gastrocnemius
muscle on the posterior surface of the lateral femoral
condyle.

9. Dissect between it and the posterolateral corner of the joint capsule; ligate or cauterize the lateral superior genicular arterial branches found in this area.

10. Make a longitudinal incision in the capsule, beginning well proximal to the joint line to avoid damaging the meniscus or the popliteus tendon. Inspect the poste-
rior half of the lateral compartment posterior to the fibular collateral ligament.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — EXTENSILE APPROACH TO THE KNEE
packs.summary: Fernandez described an extensile anterior approach to the knee based on an anterolateral approach that allows easy access to the medial and lateral compartments in the fol- lowing ways: 1. An extensive osteotomy of the tibial tuberosity allows proximal reflection of the patella, patellar tendon, and retropatellar fat pad. 2. Transecting the anterior horn and anterior portion of the coronary ligament of the medial meniscus or the lateral meniscus or both as necessary achieves adequate exposure. This approach may be used for tumor resection, liga- ment reconstruction, fracture reduction and fixation, and adult reconstructive procedures. Part or all this approach may be used as necessary to achieve the required exposure. Rigid screw fixation of the tibial tuberosity engaging the posterior...
packs.text:
Fernandez described an extensile anterior approach to the
knee based on an anterolateral approach that allows easy
access to the medial and lateral compartments in the fol-
lowing ways:

1. An extensive osteotomy of the tibial tuberosity allows proximal reflection of the patella, patellar tendon, and retropatellar fat pad.

2. Transecting the anterior horn and anterior portion of the coronary ligament of the medial meniscus or the lateral meniscus or both as necessary achieves adequate exposure.

This approach may be used for tumor resection, liga-
ment reconstruction, fracture reduction and fixation, and
adult reconstructive procedures. Part or all this approach may
be used as necessary to achieve the required exposure. Rigid
screw fixation of the tibial tuberosity engaging the posterior
cortex of the tibia allows early postoperative knee motion.

Perry et al. first reported transection of the anterior
horn of the lateral meniscus to aid exposure of lateral tib-
ial plateau fractures. Alternatively, the articular surface of
either tibial plateau can be approached with submeniscal
exposure by releasing the peripheral attachment of the
meniscus at the coronary ligament and by elevating the
meniscus, as described by Gossling and Peterson.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FERNANDEZ)
packs.summary: 1. Place the patient supine and drape the limb to allow at least 60 degrees of knee flexion. 2. Begin a lateral parapatellar incision 10 cm proximal to the lateral joint line; continue it distally along the lateral border of the patella, patellar tendon, and tibial tuberosity; and end it 15 cm distal to the lateral joint line (Fig. 1.56A). 3. Develop skin flaps deep in the subcutaneous tissue, extending medially to the anterior edge of the tibial collateral ligament and laterally, exposing the iliotibial band and the proximal origins of the anterior tibial and peroneal muscles (Fig. 1.56B). 4. To expose the lateral tibial metaphysis, detach the an- terior tibial muscle and retract it distally, and elevate the iliotibial band by dividing it transversely at the joint line or by...
packs.text:
1. Place the patient supine and drape the limb to allow at least 60 degrees of knee flexion.

2. Begin a lateral parapatellar incision 10 cm proximal to the lateral joint line; continue it distally along the lateral border of the patella, patellar tendon, and tibial tuberosity; and end it 15 cm distal to the lateral joint line (Fig. 1.56A).

3. Develop skin flaps deep in the subcutaneous tissue,
extending medially to the anterior edge of the tibial

collateral ligament and laterally, exposing the iliotibial
band and the proximal origins of the anterior tibial and
peroneal muscles (Fig. 1.56B).

4. To expose the lateral tibial metaphysis, detach the an-
terior tibial muscle and retract it distally, and elevate
the iliotibial band by dividing it transversely at the joint
line or by performing a flat osteotomy of Gerdy's tu-
bercle (Fig. 1.56C). If exposure of the posteromedial
part of the tibial metaphysis is necessary, divide the
tibial insertion of the pes anserinus or elevate it as an
osteoperiosteal flap.

5. Fernandez advocates an extended osteotomy into the tibial crest in the presence of a bicondylar tibial plateau fracture to ensure that the osteotomy fragment is securely fixed into the tibial diaphysis below the level of the frac-
ture. If indicated, a less extensive osteotomy may be used.

6. Perform an extended trapezoidal osteotomy of the tibial tuberosity as follows:

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FERNANDEZ)
packs.summary: ▪ Mark with an osteotome a site 5 cm in length, 2 cm in width proximally, and 1.5 cm in width distally. Drill three holes for later reattachment of the tibial tuberosity. ▪ Complete the osteotomy with a flat osteotome (Fig. 1.56C). 7. Elevate the tibial tuberosity and patellar tendon and incise the joint capsule transversely, medially, and laterally at the joint line. 8. Carry each limb of the capsular incision proximally to the level of the anterior border of the vastus medialis and vastus lateralis (Fig. 1.56D). 9. If further exposure of the articular surface of the tibial plateaus is needed, detach one or both menisci by transection of the anterior horn, cutting the transverse ligament, and dividing the anterior part of the coro- nary ligament. The meniscus may be elevated and held...
packs.text:
▪ Mark with an osteotome a site 5 cm in length, 2 cm in width proximally, and 1.5 cm in width distally.

Drill three holes for later reattachment of the tibial tuberosity.

▪ Complete the osteotomy with a flat osteotome
(Fig. 1.56C).

7. Elevate the tibial tuberosity and patellar tendon and incise the joint capsule transversely, medially, and laterally at the joint line.

8. Carry each limb of the capsular incision proximally to
the level of the anterior border of the vastus medialis
and vastus lateralis (Fig. 1.56D).

9. If further exposure of the articular surface of the tibial plateaus is needed, detach one or both menisci by transection of the anterior horn, cutting the transverse ligament, and dividing the anterior part of the coro-
nary ligament. The meniscus may be elevated and held with a stay suture (Fig. 1.56E).

10. At wound closure, repair the anterior meniscus, coro-
nary ligament, and transverse ligament with 2-0 non-
absorbable sutures. Use square stitches to repair the
meniscus and two or three U-shaped stitches to stabi-
lize the periphery of the meniscus.

11. Tie the stitches over the joint capsule after the closure
of the medial and lateral arthrotomies (Fig. 1.56F).

12. Reattach the anterior tibial muscle and pes anserinus to the bone with interrupted sutures.

13. Reattach Gerdy’s tubercle with a lag screw.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FERNANDEZ)
packs.summary: 14. Rigidly fix the tibial tuberosity osteotomy with lag screws, obtaining good purchase in the posterior cor- tex of the tibia. 15. Close the arthrotomy with interrupted sutures (Fig. 1.56G).
packs.text:
14. Rigidly fix the tibial tuberosity osteotomy with lag screws, obtaining good purchase in the posterior cor-
tex of the tibia.

15. Close the arthrotomy with interrupted sutures
(Fig. 1.56G).

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — DIRECT POSTERIOR AND POSTEROLATERAL APPROACHES TO THE KNEE
packs.summary: The approaches supply access to the posterior capsule of the knee joint, the posterior part of the menisci, the posterior compartments of the knee, the posterior aspect of the femo- ral and tibial condyles, and the origin of the posterior cruci- ate ligament. All posterior approaches are done with the patient prone. *FIGURE 1.56 Fernandez extensile anterior approach. A, Anterolateral incision. B, Extensor mechanism exposed. C, Iliotibial band is reflected with Gerdy's tubercle. Anterior compartment and pes anserinus are detached and elevated as necessary. Osteotomy of tibial tuberosity is outlined, and screw holes are predrilled (see text). D, Patella, patellar tendon, and tibial tuberosity are elevated. E, Medial and lateral menisci are detached anteriorly and peripherally and are...
packs.text:
The approaches supply access to the posterior capsule of the
knee joint, the posterior part of the menisci, the posterior
compartments of the knee, the posterior aspect of the femo-
ral and tibial condyles, and the origin of the posterior cruci-
ate ligament. All posterior approaches are done with the
patient prone.

*FIGURE 1.56 Fernandez extensile anterior approach. A, Anterolateral incision. B, Extensor mechanism exposed. C, Iliotibial band is reflected with Gerdy's tubercle. Anterior compartment and pes anserinus are detached and elevated as necessary. Osteotomy of tibial tuberosity is outlined, and screw holes are predrilled (see text). D, Patella, patellar tendon, and tibial tuberosity are elevated. E, Medial and lateral menisci are detached anteriorly and peripherally and are elevated. F, Meniscal repair is performed with 2-0 nonabsorbable sutures (see text). Gerdy's tubercle is reattached with lag screw. Anterior tibial and pes anserinus are reattached. G, Tibial tuberosity is secured with lag screws engaging posterior cortex of tibia. Capsule is closed with interrupted sutures. Sutures in periphery of menisci are now tied (see text). (Modified from Fernan-
dez DL: Anterior approach to the knee with osteotomy of the tibial tubercle for bicondylar tibial fractures, J Bone Joint Surg 70A:208, 1988.) SEE TECHNIQUE 1.53.*

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 1.54
packs.summary: (Brackett and Osgood; Putti; Abbott and Carpenter) 1. Make a curvilinear incision 10 to 15 cm long over the popliteal space (Fig. 1.57A), with the proximal limb following the tendon of the semitendinosus muscle distally to the level of the joint. Curve it laterally across the posterior aspect of the joint for about 5 cm and distally over the lateral head of the gastrocnemius muscle. 2. Reflect the skin and subcutaneous tissues to expose the popliteal fascia. 3. Find the posterior cutaneous nerve of the calf (the medial sural cutaneous nerve) lying beneath the fascia and between the two heads of the gastrocnemius muscle because it is the clue to the dissection. Lateral to it, the short saphenous vein perforates the popliteal fascia to join the popliteal vein at the middle of the fossa....
packs.text:
(Brackett and Osgood; Putti; Abbott and Carpenter)

1. Make a curvilinear incision 10 to 15 cm long over the popliteal space (Fig. 1.57A), with the proximal limb following the tendon of the semitendinosus muscle distally to the level of the joint. Curve it laterally across

the posterior aspect of the joint for about 5 cm and
distally over the lateral head of the gastrocnemius
muscle.

2. Reflect the skin and subcutaneous tissues to expose the popliteal fascia.

3. Find the posterior cutaneous nerve of the calf (the medial sural cutaneous nerve) lying beneath the fascia and between the two heads of the gastrocnemius muscle because it is the clue to the dissection. Lateral to it, the short saphenous vein perforates the popliteal fascia to join the popliteal vein at the middle of the fossa. Trace the posterior cutaneous nerve of the calf (the medial sural cutaneous nerve) proximally to its origin from the tibial

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 1.54
packs.summary: *FIGURE 1.57 Direct posterior approach to knee joint. A, Posterior curvilinear incision, Poste- rior cutaneous nerve of calf is exposed and retracted. B, Sciatic nerve and its division defined. C, Medial head of gastrocnemius muscle exposed. D, Tendon of origin of medial head of gastroc- nemius muscle divided, exposing capsule of knee joint. If further exposure is necessary, lateral head of gastrocnemius is defined, incised, and retracted in similar fashion. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.54.* nerve because the contents of the fossa can be dissected accurately and safely once this nerve is found. Trace the tibial nerve distally and expose its branches to the heads of the gastrocnemius, the plantaris, and the soleus mus- cles; these branches are accompanied by arteries and veins. Follow the tibial...
packs.text:
*FIGURE 1.57 Direct posterior approach to knee joint. A, Posterior curvilinear incision, Poste-
rior cutaneous nerve of calf is exposed and retracted. B, Sciatic nerve and its division defined.
C, Medial head of gastrocnemius muscle exposed. D, Tendon of origin of medial head of gastroc-
nemius muscle divided, exposing capsule of knee joint. If further exposure is necessary, lateral
head of gastrocnemius is defined, incised, and retracted in similar fashion. SEE TECHNIQUE 1.54.*

nerve because the contents of the fossa can be dissected
accurately and safely once this nerve is found. Trace the
tibial nerve distally and expose its branches to the heads
of the gastrocnemius, the plantaris, and the soleus mus-
cles; these branches are accompanied by arteries and
veins. Follow the tibial nerve proximally to the apex of
the fossa, where it joins the common peroneal nerve
(Fig. 1.57B). Dissect the common peroneal nerve distally
along the medial border of the biceps muscle and tendon,
and protect the lateral cutaneous nerve of the calf and the
anastomotic peroneal nerve. The tibial nerve and com-
mon peroneal nerve should be retracted together toward
the lateral aspect to avoid a neuropraxis injury.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 1.54
packs.summary: 4. Expose the popliteal artery and vein, which lie directly anterior and medial to the tibial nerve. Gently retract the artery and vein medially and find and trace the su- perolateral and superomedial genicular vessels passing beneath the hamstring muscles on either side just proximal to the heads of origin of the gastrocnemius (Fig. 1.58). 5. Open the posterior compartments of the joint with the knee extended, and explore them with the knee slightly flexed. The medial head of the gastrocnemius arises at a more proximal level from the femoral condyle than does the lateral head, and the groove it forms with the semi- membranosus forms a safe and comparatively avascular approach to the medial compartment (Fig. 1.57C). Turn the tendinous origin of the medial head of the gastroc- nemius...
packs.text:
4. Expose the popliteal artery and vein, which lie directly
anterior and medial to the tibial nerve. Gently retract
the artery and vein medially and find and trace the su-
perolateral and superomedial genicular vessels passing
beneath the hamstring muscles on either side just
proximal to the heads of origin of the gastrocnemius
(Fig. 1.58).

5. Open the posterior compartments of the joint with the
knee extended, and explore them with the knee slightly
flexed. The medial head of the gastrocnemius arises at a
more proximal level from the femoral condyle than does
the lateral head, and the groove it forms with the semi-
membranosus forms a safe and comparatively avascular
approach to the medial compartment (Fig. 1.57C). Turn
the tendinous origin of the medial head of the gastroc-
nemius laterally to serve as a retractor for the popliteal
vessels and nerves (Fig. 1.57D).

6. Greater access can be achieved by ligating one or more genicular vessels. If the posterolateral aspect of the joint is to be exposed, elevate the lateral head of the gastroc-
nemius muscle from the femur and approach the lateral compartment between the tendon of the biceps femo-
ris and the lateral head of the gastrocnemius muscle.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 1.54
packs.summary: 7. When closing the wound, place interrupted sutures in the capsule, the deep fascia, and the skin. The popliteal fascia is best closed by placing all sutures before draw- ing them tight. Tie the sutures one by one.
packs.text:
7. When closing the wound, place interrupted sutures in the capsule, the deep fascia, and the skin. The popliteal fascia is best closed by placing all sutures before draw-
ing them tight. Tie the sutures one by one.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — DIRECT POSTEROLATERAL APPROACH
packs.summary: TO THE KNEE Minkoff et al. described a limited posterolateral approach to the proximal lateral tibia and knee. It uses the interval between the popliteus and soleus muscles and exposes the uppermost lateral part of the posterior tibial metaphysis and the proximal tibiofibular joint. Although this approach was developed to excise an osteoid osteoma from the lateral tibial plateau, it can be used for other conditions affecting the posterior aspect of the knee.
packs.text:
TO THE KNEE

Minkoff et al. described a limited posterolateral approach
to the proximal lateral tibia and knee. It uses the interval
between the popliteus and soleus muscles and exposes the
uppermost lateral part of the posterior tibial metaphysis
and the proximal tibiofibular joint. Although this approach
was developed to excise an osteoid osteoma from the
lateral tibial plateau, it can be used for other conditions
affecting the posterior aspect of the knee.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (MINKOFF, JAFFE, AND MENENDEZ)
packs.summary: 1. Begin the skin incision 1 to 2 cm below the popliteal crease slightly medial to the midline of the knee, carrying it transversely and curving it distally just medial and parallel to the head of the fibula, ending 5 to 6 cm distally to it. 2. Reflect the skin and subcutaneous flap anteromedially. 3. Isolate the lateral cutaneous nerve of the calf, retract it laterally, and preserve it. 4. Find the short saphenous vein superficial to the fascia and divide and ligate it. 5. Open the fascia carefully in line with the incision. The sural nerve lies deep to the fascia just superficial to the heads of the gastrocnemius muscle and must be pro- tected (Fig. 1.59A). 6. Find the common peroneal nerve and retract it laterally. 7. Develop the interval between the lateral head of the...
packs.text:
1. Begin the skin incision 1 to 2 cm below the popliteal
crease slightly medial to the midline of the knee, carrying
it transversely and curving it distally just medial and parallel
to the head of the fibula, ending 5 to 6 cm distally to it.

2. Reflect the skin and subcutaneous flap anteromedially.

3. Isolate the lateral cutaneous nerve of the calf, retract it laterally, and preserve it.

4. Find the short saphenous vein superficial to the fascia
and divide and ligate it.

5. Open the fascia carefully in line with the incision. The
sural nerve lies deep to the fascia just superficial to the
heads of the gastrocnemius muscle and must be pro-
tected (Fig. 1.59A).

6. Find the common peroneal nerve and retract it laterally.

7. Develop the interval between the lateral head of the gastrocnemius and the soleus muscles, and retract the lateral head of the gastrocnemius medially.

8. Retract the popliteal artery and vein and the tibial
nerve along with the lateral head of the gastrocnemius
(Fig. 1.59B). Dissect free the fibular origin of the soleus
muscle and retract it distally.

9. Retract the underlying popliteus muscle medially to ex-
pose the posterior aspect of the lateral tibial plateau
and proximal tibiofibular joint (Fig. 1.59C).

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (MINKOFF, JAFFE, AND MENENDEZ)
packs.summary: *FIGURE 1.59 Minkoff, Jaffe, and Menendez posterolateral approach. A, Superficial dissection. B, Gastrocnemius and popliteal vessels are retracted medially, and fibular origin of soleus is reflected distally. C, Popliteus is retracted medially, exposing the posterior aspect of tibial plateau and proximal tibiofibular joint. (Modified from Minkoff J, Jaffe L, Menendez L: Limited posterolateral surgical approach to the knee for excision of osteoid osteoma, Clin Orthop Relat Res 223:237, 1987.) SEE TECHNIQUE 1.55.*

reflected distally. C, Popliteus is retracted medially, exposing the posterior aspect of tibial plateau
and proximal tibiofibular joint. (Modified from Minkoff J, Jaffe L, Menendez L: Limited posterolateral
surgical approach to the knee for excision of osteoid osteoma, Clin Orthop Relat Res 223:237, 1987.)
SEE TECHNIQUE 1.55.*

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — CAMPBELL'S CONCEPTS
packs.summary: Recommendations and Precautions in Posterior Approaches to the Knee The posterior midline approach involves structures that, if damaged, can produce a permanent, serious disability. A thorough knowledge of the anatomy of the popliteal space is essential; see Figure 1.60, which shows the relationship of the flexion crease to the joint line, and Figure 1.58, which shows the collateral circulation around the knee posteriorly.
packs.text:
Recommendations and Precautions in
Posterior Approaches to the Knee

The posterior midline approach involves structures that,
if damaged, can produce a permanent, serious disability.
A thorough knowledge of the anatomy of the popliteal
space is essential; see Figure 1.60, which shows the
relationship of the flexion crease to the joint line, and
Figure 1.58, which shows the collateral circulation
around the knee posteriorly.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PATELLOFEMORAL ARTHROPLASTY
packs.summary: Although historically controversial, new interest in patello- femoral arthroplasty has been fueled by contemporary im- plant designs that have produced improved clinical outcomes. First-generation designs failed because of narrow trochlear grooves and high constraint, which often produced maltrack- ing, patellar catching, or persistent anterior knee pain. Despite improvements in the current designs, the most common rea- son for failure of the second-generation implants is progres- sion of tibiofemoral arthritis, making careful patient selection the key to a successful outcome. The use of custom-designed implants to allow the least amount of bone loss has been re- ported. These implants, although more expensive than the off-the-shelf sizing that most manufacturers offer, are being...
packs.text:
Although historically controversial, new interest in patello-
femoral arthroplasty has been fueled by contemporary im-
plant designs that have produced improved clinical outcomes.
First-generation designs failed because of narrow trochlear
grooves and high constraint, which often produced maltrack-
ing, patellar catching, or persistent anterior knee pain. Despite
improvements in the current designs, the most common rea-
son for failure of the second-generation implants is progres-
sion of tibiofemoral arthritis, making careful patient selection
the key to a successful outcome. The use of custom-designed
implants to allow the least amount of bone loss has been re-
ported. These implants, although more expensive than the
off-the-shelf sizing that most manufacturers offer, are being
reported to have excellent outcomes and short-term to mid-
term survivorship.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PATELLOFEMORAL ARTHROPLASTY
packs.summary: The ideal candidate for patellofemoral arthroplasty is a patient who is younger than 65 years of age and has debili- tating, isolated patellofemoral arthritis with minimal coro- nal deformity; pain during daily activities is localized to the patellofemoral joint and has not responded to conservative measures. Patellofemoral arthroplasty is recommended for isolated patellofemoral OA to provide a conservative, bone- sparing alternative to TKA, which may not have as good patient satisfaction in young, active patients. Multiple re- ports have indicated that conversion of a patellofemoral arthroplasty is comparable to performing a primary TKA. In a study of 21 patellofemoral arthroplasty conversions to TKA compared with 21 primary and revision TKAs, there were more complications in the...
packs.text:
The ideal candidate for patellofemoral arthroplasty is a
patient who is younger than 65 years of age and has debili-
tating, isolated patellofemoral arthritis with minimal coro-
nal deformity; pain during daily activities is localized to the
patellofemoral joint and has not responded to conservative
measures. Patellofemoral arthroplasty is recommended for
isolated patellofemoral OA to provide a conservative, bone-
sparing alternative to TKA, which may not have as good
patient satisfaction in young, active patients. Multiple re-
ports have indicated that conversion of a patellofemoral
arthroplasty is comparable to performing a primary TKA.
In a study of 21 patellofemoral arthroplasty conversions to
TKA compared with 21 primary and revision TKAs, there
were more complications in the patellofemoral arthroplasty

conversion group. However, this was not comparable to the
revision cohort. To date there are no published studies
showing that outcomes of patellofemoral arthroplasty are
age dependent. All of these considerations should be taken
into account before offering a patellofemoral arthroplasty
over a TKA.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PATELLOFEMORAL ARTHROPLASTY
packs.summary: Good results have been reported after patellofemoral arthroplasty in patients with posttraumatic arthritis, primary patellofemoral OA, and patellofemoral dysplasia without malalignment. In patients with posttraumatic arthritis, patellofemoral arthroplasty may be considered as an alternative to patellectomy. Primary patellofemoral arthritis includes Outerbridge type IV chondromalacia of the patella or trochlea or both. Note that progression of tibiofemoral arthritis is more frequent with primary OA than with posttraumatic arthritis or patellofemoral dysplasia. Malalignment is most often determined using the quadriceps angle (Q angle). Angles of more than 15 degrees in men and 20 degrees in women are considered abnormal. Any condition that increases the Q angle increases the lateral...
packs.text: Good results have been reported after patellofemoral arthroplasty in patients with posttraumatic arthritis, primary patellofemoral OA, and patellofemoral dysplasia without malalignment. In patients with posttraumatic arthritis, patellofemoral arthroplasty may be considered as an alternative to patellectomy. Primary patellofemoral arthritis includes Outerbridge type IV chondromalacia of the patella or trochlea or both. Note that progression of tibiofemoral arthritis is more frequent with primary OA than with posttraumatic arthritis or patellofemoral dysplasia. Malalignment is most often determined using the quadriceps angle (Q angle). Angles of more than 15 degrees in men and 20 degrees in women are considered abnormal. Any condition that increases the Q angle increases the lateral displacement forces on the patella and may lead to subluxation or dislocation. Patellofemoral arthroplasty alone cannot correct patellar malalignment and/or instability. Malalignment and/or instability of the patellofemoral joint is not an indication for the procedure. Mild patellar tilt or subluxation can be corrected at the time of patellofemoral arthroplasty with lateral retinacular release, medialization of the patellar component, and possibly partial lateral facetectomy. Malalignment should be corrected before or during patellofemoral arthroplasty. No particular patellar or trochlear wear pattern

Lateral and inferior patellar facet lesions in younger patients can be treated with anterior medialization of the tibial tubercle. A recent analysis showed that patients with trochlear dysplasia tend to have an internally rotated placement of the trochlear groove and that patellofemoral arthroplasty can compensate for this pathologic alignment.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PATELLOFEMORAL ARTHROPLASTY
packs.summary: Progression of tibiofemoral arthritis is the most common reason for revision to TKA, emphasizing that tibiofemoral arthrosis is a principal contraindication to patellofemoral arthroplasty. Inflammatory arthropathies involve the entire joint and currently are a contraindication to patellofemoral arthroplasty because of progressive tibiofemoral arthritis and painful synovitis. This includes chondrocalcinosis, which can be indicative of an inflammatory arthropathy and can lead to altered joint mechanics because of abnormal menisci.

Inflammatory arthropathies involve the entire joint and currently are a contraindication to patellofemoral arthroplasty because of progressive tibiofemoral arthritis and painful synovitis. This includes chondrocalcinosis, which can be indicative of an inflammatory arthropathy and can lead to altered joint mechanics because of abnormal menisci.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PATELLOFEMORAL ARTHROPLASTY
packs.summary: Patellofemoral arthroplasty also is not indicated in pa- tients with severe coronal deformity of the knee (valgus of more than 8 degrees or varus of more than 5 degrees) unless the deformity is corrected by osteotomy before arthroplasty. Flexion of 120 degrees in the sagittal plane, with less than 10 degrees of flexion contracture, is recommended as long as the flexion contracture is not caused by OA in the medial or lateral compartment of the knee. Knee joint stiffness should be carefully assessed because this patient population has a high rate of previous surgery that increases the frequency of arthrofibrosis and patellar height abnormalities. Patients with patella baja from quadriceps muscle atrophy or patellar ten- don scarring are not good candidates for patellofemoral ar-...
packs.text:
Patellofemoral arthroplasty also is not indicated in pa-
tients with severe coronal deformity of the knee (valgus of
more than 8 degrees or varus of more than 5 degrees) unless
the deformity is corrected by osteotomy before arthroplasty.
Flexion of 120 degrees in the sagittal plane, with less than
10 degrees of flexion contracture, is recommended as long as
the flexion contracture is not caused by OA in the medial or
lateral compartment of the knee. Knee joint stiffness should
be carefully assessed because this patient population has a
high rate of previous surgery that increases the frequency of
arthrofibrosis and patellar height abnormalities. Patients with
patella baja from quadriceps muscle atrophy or patellar ten-
don scarring are not good candidates for patellofemoral ar-
throplasty. Although few data exist correlating the outcome
of patellofemoral arthroplasty with BMI, currently it is not
recommended in obese patients because of concerns about

overloading of the implant. A recent study showed a higher
rate of revision to TKA in obese patients (BMI >30) than in
nonobese patients, whereas primary diagnosis, age, or sex did
not significantly affect the revision rate.

Reported results of patellofemoral arthroplasty indi
cated that it provides excellent pain relief and functional

packs.summary: PATELLAR RESURFACING IN PRIMARY TOTAL KNEE ARTHROPLASTY The role of universal patellar resurfacing in TKA is somewhat controversial, with some advocating it because of clinical series indicating that knee scores after patellar resurfacing are slightly better because of less residual peripatellar pain and improved quadriceps strength. packs.text:
PATELLAR RESURFACING IN PRIMARY
TOTAL KNEE ARTHROPLASTY

The role of universal patellar resurfacing in TKA is somewhat controversial, with some advocating it because of clinical series indicating that knee scores after patellar resurfacing are slightly better because of less residual peripatellar pain and improved quadriceps strength. In a large retrospective study, patello-
femoral complications occurred in 4% of patients with patellar resurfacing compared with 12% of patients in whom the pa-
tella was unresurfaced. Significant residual anterior knee pain was the most common complication in the unresurfaced group. A 5-year prospective, randomized study of a single knee design found that 25% of patients with unresurfaced patellae complained of anterior knee pain, whereas only 5% of patients with patellar resurfacing complained of anterior knee pain.

Secondary resurfacing of the patella for residual anterior knee
pain after TKA has been studied by various authors who found
that pain relief after secondary resurfacing was inferior to what
would be expected with primary resurfacing and found a
higher rate of complications, including patellar fracture and
postoperative stiffness.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — INDICATIONS AND CONSIDERATIONS FOR
packs.summary: Other authors have advocated selective resurfacing of the patella. The major argument in favor of selective resurfacing of the patella is that complications of resurfaced patellae account for most of the reoperations after TKA in many series. Also, with selective resurfacing of the patella, using a femoral com- ponent that incorporates an anatomically shaped femoral trochlea, essentially equal knee scores have been reported for resurfaced and unresurfaced groups. Prospective studies com- paring TKA with and without patellar resurfacing have found no significant differences in patient preferences, functional scores, anterior knee pain, or revision rates. However, these reports have found that those with an unresurfaced patella who have secondary procedures for resurfacing are not always...
packs.text:
Other authors have advocated selective resurfacing of the patella. The major argument in favor of selective resurfacing of the patella is that complications of resurfaced patellae account for most of the reoperations after TKA in many series. Also, with selective resurfacing of the patella, using a femoral com-
ponent that incorporates an anatomically shaped femoral trochlea, essentially equal knee scores have been reported for resurfaced and unresurfaced groups. Prospective studies com-
paring TKA with and without patellar resurfacing have found no significant differences in patient preferences, functional scores, anterior knee pain, or revision rates. However, these reports have found that those with an unresurfaced patella who have secondary procedures for resurfacing are not always satisfied after secondary resurfacing. This has led some to sug-
gest that anterior knee pain after TKA is related more to com-
ponent design and improper alignment in the transverse plane than to patellar retention or resurfacing. A recent report from the Australian registry on selective resurfacing found a

higher rate of revision compared with those who routinely
resurface the patella.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — INDICATIONS AND CONSIDERATIONS FOR
packs.summary: The desirability of resurfacing continues to be debated, and the results of selective patellar resurfacing seem to be dependent on the design of the trochlear groove, with a na- tive patella articulating within an anatomic trochlear groove giving results similar to those of TKA with resurfacing of the patella. Suggested indications for leaving the patella unresur- faced are a primary diagnosis of OA, satisfactory patellar cartilage with no eburnated bone, congruent patellofemoral tracking, a normal anatomic patellar shape, and no evidence of crystalline or inflammatory arthropathy. A report using a similar implant with a resurfaced patella on one knee and an unresurfaced patella on the contralateral side found that pa- tients could not discern a difference.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — INDICATIONS AND CONSIDERATIONS FOR
packs.summary: Patient weight also seems to be a factor, with lighter pa- tients tending to do well with unresurfaced patellae. This may be one factor in the trend to routinely leave the native patella seen in the European literature. Some have suggested that resurfacing the patella in patients with BMI ≥50) may over- load the patellofemoral compartment and function as a pain generator. To date, no clinical series has been published com- paring pain scores between BMI ≥50 and lighter patients. A report of the Australian registry indicated that PS TKA de- signs with unresurfaced patellae had higher revision rates after surgery than those with resurfaced patellae. They found that the minimally constrained (CR) designs with resurfaced patellae had the lowest rate of revision followed by PS designs with...
packs.text:
Patient weight also seems to be a factor, with lighter pa-
tients tending to do well with unresurfaced patellae. This may
be one factor in the trend to routinely leave the native patella
seen in the European literature. Some have suggested that
resurfacing the patella in patients with BMI ≥50) may over-
load the patellofemoral compartment and function as a pain
generator. To date, no clinical series has been published com-
paring pain scores between BMI ≥50 and lighter patients. A
report of the Australian registry indicated that PS TKA de-
signs with unresurfaced patellae had higher revision rates
after surgery than those with resurfaced patellae. They found
that the minimally constrained (CR) designs with resurfaced
patellae had the lowest rate of revision followed by PS designs
with resurfaced patellae. In addition, onlay resurfacing fared
better than inlay patellar designed buttons. Another report
determined that resurfacing was cost-effective based on the
number of revisions reported after unresurfaced TKA.
