packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — MUSCLE CONTRACTURES
packs.summary: Contractures can develop in almost any muscle group. The cause can be obscure, as in congenital contractures, or obvi- ous, as in infection, ischemia, trauma, or injection myositis. Injection fibrosis most commonly occurs in the quadriceps muscle but also has been described in the gluteal, deltoid, and triceps muscles. Contractures in infants often follow intramuscular antibiotic injections. Intramuscular use of opioid analgesics and other narcotics also can induce mus- cular fibrosis and contracture.
packs.text:
Contractures can develop in almost any muscle group. The
cause can be obscure, as in congenital contractures, or obvi-
ous, as in infection, ischemia, trauma, or injection myositis.
Injection fibrosis most commonly occurs in the quadriceps
muscle but also has been described in the gluteal, deltoid,
and triceps muscles. Contractures in infants often follow
intramuscular antibiotic injections. Intramuscular use of
opioid analgesics and other narcotics also can induce mus-
cular fibrosis and contracture.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ■ ETIOLOGY
packs.summary: The etiology of quadriceps contracture is divided into con- genital and acquired types, and in some cases, there is a mixture of both types. The congenital type appears to have a familial component because it can occur in siblings, and it often occurs in patients of central and eastern Asian de- scent. The acquired type is seen in association with multiple injections or infusions into the thigh soon after birth. The exact mechanism causing these contractures is unclear, but suggested causes include compression of the muscle bun- dles and capillaries by the volume of medication injected and the toxicity of the drug. Whatever the cause, a delay of several years between injection and contracture is common.
packs.text:
The etiology of quadriceps contracture is divided into con-
genital and acquired types, and in some cases, there is a
mixture of both types. The congenital type appears to have
a familial component because it can occur in siblings, and it
often occurs in patients of central and eastern Asian de-
scent. The acquired type is seen in association with multiple
injections or infusions into the thigh soon after birth. The
exact mechanism causing these contractures is unclear, but
suggested causes include compression of the muscle bun-
dles and capillaries by the volume of medication injected
and the toxicity of the drug. Whatever the cause, a delay of
several years between injection and contracture is common.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ■ Signs and symptoms
packs.summary: The most common symptom is progressive, painless limita- tion of knee flexion. Hyperextension and subluxation of the knee may occur with continued growth. Normal skin creases over the knee may be absent, and a characteristic dimple may be present over the area of fibrosis, especially when the knee is flexed. Habitual dislocation of the patella is common.
packs.text:
The most common symptom is progressive, painless limita-
tion of knee flexion. Hyperextension and subluxation of the
knee may occur with continued growth. Normal skin
creases over the knee may be absent, and a characteristic
dimple may be present over the area of fibrosis, especially
when the knee is flexed. Habitual dislocation of the patella
is common.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — DIAGNOSIS
packs.summary: Radiographic changes are not apparent early, but if left un- treated, the muscle contracture can cause changes in the soft tissues and in the articular cartilage of the femur and tibia. Progressive displacement and hypoplasia of the patella can occur with long-standing quadriceps contracture. In older children with early onset of symptoms but delayed treatment, flattening of the femoral condyles, genu recurvatum, anterior dislocation of the tibia, and gross degenerative changes in the joint can be seen. The following may be involved in quadriceps contrac- ture: (1) fibrosis of the vastus intermedius muscle tying down the rectus femoris to the femur in the suprapatellar pouch and proximally, (2) adhesions between the patella and the femoral condyles, (3) fibrosis and shortening of the...
packs.text:
Radiographic changes are not apparent early, but if left un-
treated, the muscle contracture can cause changes in the soft
tissues and in the articular cartilage of the femur and tibia.
Progressive displacement and hypoplasia of the patella can
occur with long-standing quadriceps contracture. In older
children with early onset of symptoms but delayed treatment,
flattening of the femoral condyles, genu recurvatum, anterior
dislocation of the tibia, and gross degenerative changes in the
joint can be seen.

The following may be involved in quadriceps contrac-
ture: (1) fibrosis of the vastus intermedius muscle tying
down the rectus femoris to the femur in the suprapatellar
pouch and proximally, (2) adhesions between the patella
and the femoral condyles, (3) fibrosis and shortening of the
lateral expansions of the vasti and their adherence to the
femoral condyles, and (4) actual shortening of the rectus
femoris muscle.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TREATMENT
packs.summary: Early recognition and prevention of quadriceps contracture through passive exercise in children receiving intramuscular injections is crucial. When the scar contracture is well estab- lished, however, surgical treatment is indicated to prevent late changes in the femoral condyles and the patella. Surgical treatment is indicated early in patients with habitual disloca- tion of the patella. To correct the deformity, Thompson devised an opera- tion known as “quadricepsplasty.” Its success depends on (1) whether the rectus femoris muscle has escaped injury, (2) how well this muscle can be isolated from the scarred parts of the quadriceps mechanism, and (3) how well the muscle can be developed by active use. During the early stage of contracture, when no significant joint changes have...
packs.text:
Early recognition and prevention of quadriceps contracture
through passive exercise in children receiving intramuscular
injections is crucial. When the scar contracture is well estab-
lished, however, surgical treatment is indicated to prevent late
changes in the femoral condyles and the patella. Surgical
treatment is indicated early in patients with habitual disloca-
tion of the patella.

To correct the deformity, Thompson devised an opera-
tion known as “quadricepsplasty.” Its success depends on
(1) whether the rectus femoris muscle has escaped injury,
(2) how well this muscle can be isolated from the scarred
parts of the quadriceps mechanism, and (3) how well the
muscle can be developed by active use.

During the early stage of contracture, when no significant
joint changes have occurred, proximal release has been rec-
ommended to eliminate extensor lag and hemarthrosis of the
knee. When more extensive changes are apparent, a Thomp-
son type of quadricepsplasty is indicated. When genu recur-
vatum has developed, a supracondylar femoral osteotomy
(see Chapter 24 e-edition) can restore some flexion if severe
degenerative changes have occurred. Arthrodesis may be in-
dicated if symptoms are severe.

Moderate contractures, before significant bony changes
have occurred, are treated best with a proximal release of the
quadriceps (Fig. 10.1).

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (SENGUPTA)
packs.summary: 1. Make a curved incision along the base of the greater trochanter and vertically downward along the lateral aspect of the thigh for a variable distance, depending on the extent of fibrosis (Fig. 10.1A). 2. Through the upper part of the incision, section the ilio- tibial band transversely. Often the iliotibial band is thick- ened and fibrotic, contributing to the contracture. 3. Expose the upper attachment of the vastus lateralis below the greater trochanter (Fig. 10.1B). Detach the origin of the vastus lateralis from the trochanteric line and distally along the lateral intermuscular septum (Fig. 10.1C). 4. As the vastus lateralis retracts to expose the vastus intermedius, use a periosteal elevator to release the vastus intermedius from the femoral surface. 5. Flex the knee and release...
packs.text:
1. Make a curved incision along the base of the greater trochanter and vertically downward along the lateral aspect of the thigh for a variable distance, depending on the extent of fibrosis (Fig. 10.1A).

2. Through the upper part of the incision, section the ilio-
tibial band transversely. Often the iliotibial band is thick-
ened and fibrotic, contributing to the contracture.

3. Expose the upper attachment of the vastus lateralis below the greater trochanter (Fig. 10.1B). Detach the origin of the vastus lateralis from the trochanteric line and distally along the lateral intermuscular septum (Fig. 10.1C).

4. As the vastus lateralis retracts to expose the vastus intermedius, use a periosteal elevator to release the vastus intermedius from the femoral surface.

5. Flex the knee and release any remaining adhesions.

6. If the rectus component also is contracted, expose its
origin at the upper part of the incision and detach it,

*FIGURE 10.1 Sengupta proximal release of quadriceps (see text). A, Incision. B, Iliotibial
and tensor fasciae latae are cut to expose vastus lateralis, which is released along its origin.
Vastus origin is detached from trochanteric line and distally along lateral intermuscular
otum. D, If necessary, rectus femoris is released. SEE TECHNIQUE 10.1.*

after identifying and retracting the femoral nerve
(Fig. 10.1D).

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (SENGUPTA)
packs.summary: 7. Full knee flexion should be possible; release of the joint capsule usually is unnecessary in children. 8. Close the wound in routine fashion and apply a poste- rior splint with the knee in maximal flexion. POSTOPERATIVE CARE The splint is worn until all tenderness has disappeared, usually 3 to 4 weeks, and then vigorous quadriceps exercises are begun. Extension lag improves rapidly, and the child usually can walk in 4 weeks and stand up from a squatting position in 3 months. Knee stretching exercises should be continued throughout growth to prevent recurrence of the contracture.
packs.text:
7. Full knee flexion should be possible; release of the joint capsule usually is unnecessary in children.

8. Close the wound in routine fashion and apply a poste-
rior splint with the knee in maximal flexion.

POSTOPERATIVE CARE The splint is worn until all tenderness has disappeared, usually 3 to 4 weeks, and then vigorous quadriceps exercises are begun. Extension lag improves rapidly, and the child usually can walk in 4 weeks and stand up from a squatting position in 3 months. Knee stretching exercises should be continued throughout growth to prevent recurrence of the contracture.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — Quadricepsplasty for post-traumatic contracture of the knee
packs.summary: This procedure has shown good to excellent results in most patients. A combination procedure with manipulation and percutaneous pie-crusting of the distal and lateral quadri- ceps with an 18-gauge needle with the knee in flexion also has shown good results with an average gain of 70 degrees of flexion. Preoperative range of motion, number of previ- ous surgeries, duration of extension contracture, and body mass index influence the final flexion that can be achieved.
packs.text: This procedure has shown good to excellent results in most patients. A combination procedure with manipulation and percutaneous pie-crusting of the distal and lateral quadri- ceps with an 18-gauge needle with the knee in flexion also has shown good results with an average gain of 70 degrees of flexion. Preoperative range of motion, number of previ- ous surgeries, duration of extension contracture, and body mass index influence the final flexion that can be achieved.

metadata.section: Quadricepsplasty for post-traumatic contracture of the knee

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (MODIFIED THOMPSON QUADRICEPSPLASTY AS DESCRIBED BY HAHN ET AL.)
packs.summary: 1. Perform the procedure using a tourniquet. 2. Make medial and lateral parapatellar incisions for arthrolysis (Fig. 10.2A). 3. Flex the knee, and if adequate flexion is not obtained, make an anterolateral or lateral incision in the distal two-thirds of the thigh and release adhesions around the quadriceps muscle. 4. Divide the tensor fascia lata transversely in the distal thigh. Isolate the vastus lateralis from the rectus femoris and release it close to its patellar insertion. Release the vastus medialis from the rectus femoris by blunt dissec- tion (Fig. 10.2B). 5. Free the rectus femoris from the vastus intermedius, the anterior surface of the femur, and the upper pole of the patella (Fig. 10.2C). 6. Perform a Z-plasty lengthening of the rectus femoris tendon if satisfactory...
packs.text:
1. Perform the procedure using a tourniquet.

2. Make medial and lateral parapatellar incisions for arthrolysis (Fig. 10.2A).

3. Flex the knee, and if adequate flexion is not obtained,
make an anterolateral or lateral incision in the distal
two-thirds of the thigh and release adhesions around
the quadriceps muscle.

4. Divide the tensor fascia lata transversely in the distal
thigh. Isolate the vastus lateralis from the rectus femoris
and release it close to its patellar insertion. Release the
vastus medialis from the rectus femoris by blunt dissec-
tion (Fig. 10.2B).

5. Free the rectus femoris from the vastus intermedius, the anterior surface of the femur, and the upper pole of the patella (Fig. 10.2C).

6. Perform a Z-plasty lengthening of the rectus femoris tendon if satisfactory flexion has not been achieved
(Fig. 10.2D).

POSTOPERATIVE CARE The extremity is immobilized
in a splint in about 50 degrees less than the maximal
flexion obtained during surgery; this is maintained for
2 to 3 days. The extremity is then placed in a continuous
passive motion machine. Passive and active exercises for
the quadriceps and hamstrings continue and are crucial
to the success of this procedure. The knee is kept in full

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)
metadata.section: (MODIFIED THOMPSON QUADRICEPSPLASTY AS DESCRIBED BY HAHN ET AL.)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (MODIFIED THOMPSON QUADRICEPSPLASTY AS DESCRIBED BY HAHN ET AL.)
packs.summary: *FIGURE 10.2 Modified Thompson quadricepsplasty. A, Parapatellar and anterolateral inci- sions. B, Release of rectus femoris (RF) from vastus lateralis (VL) and vastus medialis (VM) close to its patellar insertion. C, Vastus intermedius (VI) and scar adhesions dissected and released from rectus femoris, anterior surface of femur, and upper pole of patella. D, RF lengthened by Z-plasty if necessary. (From Hahn SB, Lee WS, Han DY: A modified Thompson quadricepsplasty for the stiff knee. J Bone Joint Surg 82B:992, 2000. Copyright British Editorial Society of Bone and Joint Surgery.) SEE TECHNIQUE 10.2.* extension during the night and is exercised during the day with active and active-assisted exercises. If 90 degrees of flexion is not obtained after 3 months, gentle manipula- tion with...
packs.text:
*FIGURE 10.2 Modified Thompson quadricepsplasty. A, Parapatellar and anterolateral inci-
sions. B, Release of rectus femoris (RF) from vastus lateralis (VL) and vastus medialis (VM) close
to its patellar insertion. C, Vastus intermedius (VI) and scar adhesions dissected and released
from rectus femoris, anterior surface of femur, and upper pole of patella. D, RF lengthened by
Z-plasty if necessary. (From Hahn SB, Lee WS, Han DY: A modified Thompson quadricepsplasty for the
stiff knee. J Bone Joint Surg 82B:992, 2000. Copyright British Editorial Society of Bone and Joint Surgery.)
SEE TECHNIQUE 10.2.*

extension during the night and is exercised during the day with active and active-assisted exercises. If 90 degrees of flexion is not obtained after 3 months, gentle manipula-
tion with the patient under anesthesia may be required.
The patient should expect slow return of active quadriceps extension. Most patients can expect improvement in range of motion of the knee after quadricepsplasty but should expect severe quadriceps weakness for many months. If the patient is not skeletally mature, some of the improvement in flexion may be lost as growth occurs.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FLEXION, EXTENSION, AND COMBINED CONTRACTURES
packs.summary: Post-traumatic stiffness can be caused by intraarticular ad- hesions, fibrosis of the surrounding soft tissues, or both. Flexion contractures can be caused by anterior bony im- pingement or posterior periarticular adhesions. Extension contractures can be the result of posterior bony impinge- ment or anterior periarticular adhesions. Bony impinge- ments need to be treated first followed by soft-tissue releases. Regardless, the exact cause(s) of contracture should be determined before surgery. Any complex regional pain syn- drome should be controlled and surgery delayed if the patient is in the active phase. Associated fractures must be healed. A thorough workup is necessary and may include a computed tomography (CT) arthrogram, magnetic reso- nance imaging (MRI), bone scan, and plain...
packs.text:
Post-traumatic stiffness can be caused by intraarticular ad-
hesions, fibrosis of the surrounding soft tissues, or both.
Flexion contractures can be caused by anterior bony im-
pingement or posterior periarticular adhesions. Extension
contractures can be the result of posterior bony impinge-
ment or anterior periarticular adhesions. Bony impinge-
ments need to be treated first followed by soft-tissue releases.
Regardless, the exact cause(s) of contracture should be
determined before surgery. Any complex regional pain syn-
drome should be controlled and surgery delayed if the
patient is in the active phase. Associated fractures must be
healed. A thorough workup is necessary and may include a
computed tomography (CT) arthrogram, magnetic reso-
nance imaging (MRI), bone scan, and plain radiographs.

A combination of open and arthroscopic procedures may be
necessary for success (Fig. 10.3).

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ARTHROSCOPIC LYSIS OF ADHESIONS
packs.summary: FOR ARTHROFIBROSIS Arthrofibrosis is common after intraarticular knee pro- cedures and is more common after open as compared to arthroscopic procedures. For patients with knee stiffness after a procedure involving the knee joint, arthroscopic lysis of adhesions alone or combined with an open approach may be helpful in restoring a more normal range of motion. Manipulation under anesthesia also may be considered, but care should be taken when performing this procedure to minimize the risk of fracture or tendon injury. When per- forming this procedure, a tourniquet should be applied but does not necessarily need to be used unless bleeding is encountered and cannot be controlled using electrocautery.
packs.text:
FOR ARTHROFIBROSIS

Arthrofibrosis is common after intraarticular knee pro-
cedures and is more common after open as compared to
arthroscopic procedures. For patients with knee stiffness
after a procedure involving the knee joint, arthroscopic lysis
of adhesions alone or combined with an open approach
may be helpful in restoring a more normal range of motion.
Manipulation under anesthesia also may be considered, but
care should be taken when performing this procedure to
minimize the risk of fracture or tendon injury. When per-
forming this procedure, a tourniquet should be applied
but does not necessarily need to be used unless bleeding is
encountered and cannot be controlled using electrocautery.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 10.3
packs.summary: 1. Position the patient supine with a thigh post to allow a valgus moment for examination of the medial compartment. A thigh post is preferable to a leg holder for this procedure in case manipulation under anesthesia needs to be performed and for work through accessory superior lateral and superior medial portals when necessary.
packs.text: 1. Position the patient supine with a thigh post to allow a valgus moment for examination of the medial compartment. A thigh post is preferable to a leg holder for this procedure in case manipulation under anesthesia needs to be performed and for work through accessory superior lateral and superior medial portals when necessary.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FIGURE 10.3
packs.summary: Algorithm used by the Versailles (France) Orthopedic and Trauma Surgery De- partment for post-traumatic knee stiffness without osteoarthritis or intraarticular malunion. TT, Tibial tubercle. (From Pujol N, Boisrenoult P, Beaufils P: Post-traumatic knee stiffness: Surgical techniques, Orthop Traumatol Surg Res 101:S179, 2015.) 2. Insufflate the knee joint via the superolateral portal position using an 18-gauge needle and sterile 0.9% saline. NOTE! Arthrofibrotic knees may not have as much capacity for fluid as a native knee. Ensure that the needle is in the joint by observing backflow from the needle. Insufflate the knee until no more fluid can be inserted easily, typically 50 to 200 mL, depending on joint size and amount of fibrosis. 3. Establish an anterolateral portal in standard...
packs.text:
Algorithm used by the Versailles (France) Orthopedic and Trauma Surgery De-

partment for post-traumatic knee stiffness without osteoarthritis or intraarticular malunion. TT,
Tibial tubercle. (From Pujol N, Boisrenoult P, Beaufils P: Post-traumatic knee stiffness: Surgical techniques,
Orthop Traumatol Surg Res 101:S179, 2015.)

2. Insufflate the knee joint via the superolateral portal position using an 18-gauge needle and sterile 0.9% saline. NOTE! Arthrofibrotic knees may not have as much capacity for fluid as a native knee. Ensure that the needle is in the joint by observing backflow from the needle. Insufflate the knee until no more fluid can be inserted easily, typically 50 to 200 mL, depending on joint size and amount of fibrosis.

3. Establish an anterolateral portal in standard fashion.
Incise the skin and capsule using a No. 11 blade, tak-
ing care to avoid the articular cartilage of the antero-
lateral femoral condyle. Insert the blunt trocar into
the suprapatellar pouch, and carefully perform sweeps
into the suprapatellar pouch, lateral gutter, medial
gutter, and anterior interval to bluntly release adhe-
sions. Take care to protect the articular cartilage while
performing these sweeps.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FIGURE 10.3
packs.summary: 4. Insert the arthroscope into the knee. Release adhe- sions within the suprapatellar pouch using a combina- tion of electrocautery and motorized shaver. Release adhesions until the quadriceps musculature is visible. 5. Assess the medial and lateral gutters for adhesions. Release adhesions using a combination of motorized shaver and electrocautery. It is often helpful to establish accessory superior lateral and superior medial portals as either working portals or viewing portals while working in the medial and lateral gutters. 6. If patellar mobility is limited, perform medial and/or lateral retinacular releases using an electrocautery wand, taking care to protect the overlying skin. Retinacular releases may be performed from the proximal pole of the patella distally to the level of...
packs.text:
4. Insert the arthroscope into the knee. Release adhe-
sions within the suprapatellar pouch using a combina-
tion of electrocautery and motorized shaver. Release
adhesions until the quadriceps musculature is visible.

5. Assess the medial and lateral gutters for adhesions.
Release adhesions using a combination of motorized shaver and electrocautery. It is often helpful to establish accessory superior lateral and superior medial portals as either working portals or viewing portals while working in the medial and lateral gutters.

6. If patellar mobility is limited, perform medial and/or lateral retinacular releases using an electrocautery wand, taking care to protect the overlying skin. Retinacular releases may be performed from the proximal pole of the patella distally to the level of the anterolateral and anteromedial portals.

7. Flex the knee to 90 degrees and view the intercondylar notch. There is often scar tissue anterior to the anterior cruciate ligament. This can be removed using a combi-
nation of motorized shaver, biters, and judicious use of electrocautery. Take care to protect the anterior cruci-
ate ligament, as well as the anterior horns of the me-
dial and lateral menisci. Remove soft tissue until the
structures are adequately seen.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FIGURE 10.3
packs.summary: 8. In the event of a mechanical block to extension from anterior soft-tissue impingement, an anterior interval re- lease can be performed. Viewing from the anterolateral portal, use a motorized shaver to dissect anterior to the anterior horn of the medial meniscus. Take care to pro- tect the patellar tendon. Develop the interval between the anterior aspect of the tibia and patellar tendon using a combination of a motorized shaver and careful use of electrocautery to obtain hemostasis. Use a small perios- teal elevator or blunt trocar to create space in this area. 9. Inspect the medial and lateral compartments and treat concomitant pathology in standard fashion. 10. Perform a modified Gillquist maneuver to access the posterior medial compartment. In the setting of a posterior capsular...
packs.text:
8. In the event of a mechanical block to extension from anterior soft-tissue impingement, an anterior interval re-
lease can be performed. Viewing from the anterolateral
portal, use a motorized shaver to dissect anterior to the
anterior horn of the medial meniscus. Take care to pro-
tect the patellar tendon. Develop the interval between
the anterior aspect of the tibia and patellar tendon using
a combination of a motorized shaver and careful use of
electrocautery to obtain hemostasis. Use a small perios-
teal elevator or blunt trocar to create space in this area.

9. Inspect the medial and lateral compartments and treat concomitant pathology in standard fashion.

10. Perform a modified Gillquist maneuver to access the posterior medial compartment. In the setting of a posterior capsular contracture, which can limit extension, an accessory posterior medial portal may be established. Our preference is to use a cannulated spinal needle and sequentially dilate the portal until a 6-mm cannula can be inserted into the posteromedial compartment of the knee. Using a cannula allows for easy

passage of instruments in and out of the posterome-
dial portal. Extend the posteromedial capsular release
to the septum using an electrocautery wand. NOTE!
Care must be taken as dissection is carried laterally to
protect the posterior neurovascular structures.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FIGURE 10.3
packs.summary: POSTOPERATIVE CARE Immediate range of motion is a priority following arthroscopic lysis of adhesions and manipulation, and we advise physical therapy to begin on postoperative day 1. We recommend physical therapy to occur at a frequency of at least three times per week with the patient performing home exercises as well. Weight bearing is typically allowed as tolerated so long as no concomitant injuries are present. Continuous passive motion machines can be used postoperatively but are optional.
packs.text: POSTOPERATIVE CARE Immediate range of motion is a priority following arthroscopic lysis of adhesions and manipulation, and we advise physical therapy to begin on postoperative day 1. We recommend physical therapy to occur at a frequency of at least three times per week with the patient performing home exercises as well. Weight bearing is typically allowed as tolerated so long as no concomitant injuries are present. Continuous passive motion machines can be used postoperatively but are optional.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — SNAPPING SYNDROMES
packs.summary: It is common for a patient to hear or feel snapping or popping of joints. Disability or pain sufficient to justify surgery for this is rare. Most patients respond favorably to reassurance and avoidance of the specific activity that produces the snapping sensation. Snapping symptoms are rare in the knee. True snapping of the knee is extraarticular. Intraarticular catching or locking in the knee usually is caused by meniscal tears, meniscal cysts, plica bands, loose bodies, patellofemoral disorders, scar tissue from prior surgery, or arthritic joint changes. Snapping of the knee can occur in patients with an abnormal anterior inser- tion of the biceps femoris tendon on the fibular head. This can be treated by reinsertion of the tendon (Fig. 10.4) or resection of the fibular head with...
packs.text:
It is common for a patient to hear or feel snapping or popping
of joints. Disability or pain sufficient to justify surgery for this
is rare. Most patients respond favorably to reassurance and
avoidance of the specific activity that produces the snapping
sensation.

Snapping symptoms are rare in the knee. True snapping of
the knee is extraarticular. Intraarticular catching or locking in
the knee usually is caused by meniscal tears, meniscal cysts,
plica bands, loose bodies, patellofemoral disorders, scar tissue
from prior surgery, or arthritic joint changes. Snapping of the
knee can occur in patients with an abnormal anterior inser-
tion of the biceps femoris tendon on the fibular head. This can
be treated by reinsertion of the tendon (Fig. 10.4) or resection
of the fibular head with reattachment of the tendon if conser-
vative treatment fails. Biceps femoris snapping caused by a
fibular exostosis has been reported, as well as snapping from a
direct injury to the tendon. Snapping caused by direct injury
can be treated by rerouting the tendon insertion through a
tunnel in the fibular head.

The popliteus tendon can cause snapping of the knee,
which usually is palpable midway between the lateral epicon-
dyle and lateral joint line. If conservative treatment fails, a
popliteus release or tenodesis of the popliteus tendon to the
fibular collateral ligament can be done.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — SNAPPING SYNDROMES
packs.summary: Knee snapping also can be caused by abnormal insertion of the semitendinosus tendon, causing it to snap over a prominence on the medial tibial condyle. This can be treated by dividing the semitendinosus tendon at its insertion and transferring it to the semimembranosus tendon or simply by excising the hamstring tendon in the same manner as would be done for a graft harvest. A hamstring tendon sliding over an osteochondroma of the femur likewise can cause snap- ping, and excision of this generally benign lesion is indicated if symptomatic. Dynamic ultrasonography can be used to diagnose the cause of mechanical snapping.
packs.text:
Knee snapping also can be caused by abnormal insertion
of the semitendinosus tendon, causing it to snap over a
prominence on the medial tibial condyle. This can be treated
by dividing the semitendinosus tendon at its insertion and
transferring it to the semimembranosus tendon or simply by
excising the hamstring tendon in the same manner as would
be done for a graft harvest. A hamstring tendon sliding over
an osteochondroma of the femur likewise can cause snap-
ping, and excision of this generally benign lesion is indicated
if symptomatic. Dynamic ultrasonography can be used to
diagnose the cause of mechanical snapping.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PAINFUL PARA-ARTICULAR CALCIFICATIONS
packs.summary: presence of calcification in the tibial collateral ligament, as in Pellegrini–Stieda disease, usually is more directly related to trauma, such as a sprain or tear of the tibial collateral liga- ment. The treatment is the same as that for a calcification around the shoulder (see Chapter 51). Spontaneous recovery may occur without treatment, and the deposit may partially or completely resorb over time. Infiltration with a local anesthetic agent, supplemented, if desired, by injection of 40 mg of methylprednisolone (Depo- Medrol) or its equivalent, produces immediate relief and can be curative. Ultrasound and extracorporeal shock wave ther- apy also have been reported to be of benefit. The calcific de- posit may be excised if response to nonoperative measures is unsatisfactory.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TENDINITIS
packs.summary: In the evaluation of patients with tendinitis of the lower ex- tremity, a careful history of work conditions and exercise routines is necessary. Overuse (repetitive activity) or over- load (sudden increase in activity) often accentuates tendini- tis. Tendinitis from these causes usually responds to rest or avoidance of exacerbating activities, ice, the use of a compres- sive sleeve, oral and/or topical antiinflammatory medica- tions, and alterations in work or exercise habits. Mechanical abnormalities, leg-length inequality, and leg malalignment may respond to the use of a properly fitted shoe orthosis. Muscle imbalance should be treated with appropriate flexibili- ty and strengthening exercise programs.
packs.text:
In the evaluation of patients with tendinitis of the lower ex-
tremity, a careful history of work conditions and exercise
routines is necessary. Overuse (repetitive activity) or over-
load (sudden increase in activity) often accentuates tendini-
tis. Tendinitis from these causes usually responds to rest or
avoidance of exacerbating activities, ice, the use of a compres-
sive sleeve, oral and/or topical antiinflammatory medica-
tions, and alterations in work or exercise habits. Mechanical
abnormalities, leg-length inequality, and leg malalignment
may respond to the use of a properly fitted shoe orthosis.
Muscle imbalance should be treated with appropriate flexibili-
ty and strengthening exercise programs.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — SEMIMEMBRANOSUS TENDINITIS
packs.summary: Semimembranosus tendinitis can be primary or secondary. Primary isolated semimembranosus tendinitis generally oc- curs in younger, athletic patients with overuse injuries. Secondary tendinitis is related more to degenerative medial meniscal tears, degenerative changes of the medial com- partment, or chondromalacia of the patella resulting in gait alterations and overuse of the tendon. Semimembranosus tendinitis can be diagnosed by eliciting point tenderness over the posteromedial corner of the knee just distal to the joint line. The tendon usually is easily palpated. A provoca- tive test of resisted internal tibial rotation of a knee flexed at 90 degrees also may pinpoint the pain. A negative McMurray test in the absence of tenderness over the joint line can help rule out a torn...
packs.text:
Semimembranosus tendinitis can be primary or secondary.
Primary isolated semimembranosus tendinitis generally oc-
curs in younger, athletic patients with overuse injuries.
Secondary tendinitis is related more to degenerative medial
meniscal tears, degenerative changes of the medial com-
partment, or chondromalacia of the patella resulting in gait

alterations and overuse of the tendon. Semimembranosus
tendinitis can be diagnosed by eliciting point tenderness
over the posteromedial corner of the knee just distal to the
joint line. The tendon usually is easily palpated. A provoca-
tive test of resisted internal tibial rotation of a knee flexed at
90 degrees also may pinpoint the pain. A negative McMurray
test in the absence of tenderness over the joint line can help
rule out a torn meniscus or osteonecrosis of the tibial pla-
teau. Semimembranosus tendinitis generally responds to
conservative therapy. If conservative treatment fails, bone
scanning or MRI can be helpful to confirm the diagnosis.
Occasionally, a semitendinosus tendon transfer may be nec-
essary and is the treatment of choice. For secondary tendini-
tis, evaluation and treatment of the intraarticular disorder at
the time of treatment of the semimembranosus tendinitis is
recommended.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 10.4
packs.summary: (RAY, CLANCY, AND LEMON) 1. Make a 10-cm longitudinal posteromedial incision over the direct head insertion of the semimembranosus tendon, and free the tendon from surrounding tissue proximally and distally. 2. Open the tendon sheath and make a longitudinal inci- sion in the tendon. 3. Excise any necrotic areas. If there is no obvious necrosis, make several longitudinal incisions in the tendon to stimulate a healing reaction. 4. Drill the insertion site with a small Kirschner wire. 5. Pull the direct head and proximal portion of the tendon upward and parallel to the posterior edge of the tibial collateral ligament and suture it here to redirect the tendon. POSTOPERATIVE CARE Postoperative care is the same as for Technique 10.7.
packs.text:
(RAY, CLANCY, AND LEMON)

1. Make a 10-cm longitudinal posteromedial incision over the direct head insertion of the semimembranosus tendon, and free the tendon from surrounding tissue proximally and distally.

2. Open the tendon sheath and make a longitudinal inci-
sion in the tendon.

3. Excise any necrotic areas. If there is no obvious necrosis,
make several longitudinal incisions in the tendon to
stimulate a healing reaction.

4. Drill the insertion site with a small Kirschner wire.

5. Pull the direct head and proximal portion of the tendon upward and parallel to the posterior edge of the tibial collateral ligament and suture it here to redirect the tendon.

POSTOPERATIVE CARE Postoperative care is the same
as for Technique 10.7.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — BURSITIS
packs.summary: Bursae are sacs lined with a membrane similar to synovium; they usually are located around joints or where skin, tendon, or muscle moves over a bony prominence, and they may or may not communicate with a joint. Their function is to reduce friction and to protect delicate structures from pressure. Bur- sae are similar to tendon sheaths and the synovial membranes of joints and are subject to the same disturbances, acute or chronic trauma, acute or chronic pyogenic infection, and low- grade inflammatory conditions such as gout, syphilis, tuber- culosis, or rheumatoid arthritis. There are more than 140 bursae in the human body. Bursae consist of two types: those normally present (e.g., over the patella and olecranon) and adventitious ones (e.g., develop over a bunion, an osteocho- droma,...
packs.text:
Bursae are sacs lined with a membrane similar to synovium; they usually are located around joints or where skin, tendon, or muscle moves over a bony prominence, and they may or may not communicate with a joint. Their function is to reduce friction and to protect delicate structures from pressure. Bur-
sae are similar to tendon sheaths and the synovial membranes of joints and are subject to the same disturbances, acute or chronic trauma, acute or chronic pyogenic infection, and low-
grade inflammatory conditions such as gout, syphilis, tuber-
culosis, or rheumatoid arthritis. There are more than 140 bursae in the human body. Bursae consist of two types: those normally present (e.g., over the patella and olecranon) and adventitious ones (e.g., develop over a bunion, an osteocho-
droma, or kyphosis of the spine). Adventitious bursae are produced by repeated trauma or constant friction or pressure. These type bursae have a much thicker fibrous wall than nor-
mal bursae and are more susceptible to inflammatory changes.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — Infected Bursae
packs.summary: ▪ Identification of responsible organism if possible ▪ Administration of appropriate antibiotics ▪ Aspiration of bursa and injection of the appropriate antibiotic may be indicated followed by a compression dressing ▪ Surgical drainage occasionally if unresponsive to aspira- tion and culture-directed antibiotics

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — Traumatic Bursitis
packs.summary: ▪ Nonoperative treatment consisting of ice, rest, antiin- flammatory medication, and protection with external padding ▪ Aspiration and injection of an appropriate steroid preparation if symptoms do not respond to nonoperative treatment

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — Adventitious Bursitis
packs.summary: ▪ Removal of cause (e.g., osteochondroma excision) ▪ Excision of bursal sac Treatment is determined primarily by the cause of the bursitis and only secondarily by the pathologic change in the bursa. Surgery is not required in most instances. Systemic causes, such as gout or syphilis, and local trauma or irritants should be eliminated, and when necessary, the patient's occupation or posture should be changed. Bursitis may respond to rest, ice, moist heat, elevation, protective padding, and, if necessary, immobilization of the affected part. Procedures useful in treating bursitis are (1) aspiration and injection of an appropriate steroid preparation or anti- biotic, (2) incision and drainage when an acute suppurative bursitis fails to respond to nonsurgical treatment, (3) exci- sion of...
packs.text:
▪ Removal of cause (e.g., osteochondroma excision)

▪ Excision of bursal sac

Treatment is determined primarily by the cause of the bursitis and only secondarily by the pathologic change in the bursa. Surgery is not required in most instances. Systemic causes, such as gout or syphilis, and local trauma or irritants should be eliminated, and when necessary, the patient's occupation or posture should be changed. Bursitis may respond to rest, ice, moist heat, elevation, protective padding, and, if necessary, immobilization of the affected part. Procedures useful in treating bursitis are (1) aspiration and injection of an appropriate steroid preparation or anti-
biotic, (2) incision and drainage when an acute suppurative bursitis fails to respond to nonsurgical treatment, (3) exci-
sion of chronically infected and thickened bursae, and (4) removal of an underlying bony prominence (Box 10.1).
Only bursae that most often require surgical drainage or excision are described.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — PREPATELLAR BURSITIS
packs.summary: Traumatic prepatellar bursitis (Fig. 10.6) can be caused by an acute injury, such as a fall directly onto the patella, or by recurrent minor injuries, such as those that produce “housemaid’s knee.” This disease entity is seen frequently in grappling sports such as wrestling. Either type usually responds to conservative treatment. If fibrosis or synovial thickening with painful nodules fails to respond to such treatment, however, excision of the bursa is indicated. Excision may be performed endoscopically or through an open approach. Pyogenic prepatellar bursitis is relatively common, es- pecially in children. If the bursa is unusually large, the swelling may be so pronounced that a diagnosis of pyo- genic arthritis of the knee joint can be made by mistake. A careful physical...
packs.text:
Traumatic prepatellar bursitis (Fig. 10.6) can be caused by
an acute injury, such as a fall directly onto the patella, or
by recurrent minor injuries, such as those that produce
“housemaid’s knee.” This disease entity is seen frequently
in grappling sports such as wrestling. Either type usually
responds to conservative treatment. If fibrosis or synovial
thickening with painful nodules fails to respond to such
treatment, however, excision of the bursa is indicated.
Excision may be performed endoscopically or through an
open approach.

Pyogenic prepatellar bursitis is relatively common, es-
pecially in children. If the bursa is unusually large, the
swelling may be so pronounced that a diagnosis of pyo-
genic arthritis of the knee joint can be made by mistake. A
careful physical examination should lead to the correct di-
agnosis. This septic prepatellar bursitis often responds to
one or two daily aspirations, appropriate immobilization,
and antibiotic coverage. If symptoms have not improved
significantly in 36 to 48 hours, incision and drainage
should be done.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — FIGURE 10.6
packs.summary: Multiple bursae around knee that may become acutely or chronically inflamed.
packs.text:
Multiple bursae around knee that may become

acutely or chronically inflamed.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 10.5
packs.summary: 1. Approach the bursa through two longitudinal incisions, one medial and one lateral, or through a single trans- verse incision. 2. Open the bursa, evacuate its contents, and pack it loosely with petrolatum gauze, or close it loosely over a drain as seems appropriate.
packs.text:
1. Approach the bursa through two longitudinal incisions, one medial and one lateral, or through a single trans-
verse incision.

2. Open the bursa, evacuate its contents, and pack it loosely with petrolatum gauze, or close it loosely over a drain as seems appropriate.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — POSTOPERATIVE CARE
packs.summary: Because cellulitis is usually present, the extremity is immobilized in a posterior splint, and appropriate antibiotics are given. If gauze has been used to pack the bursa, it is changed at least twice weekly. Despite sufficient drainage, sinuses often persist for a time on one or both sides of the knee. Immobiliza- tion is continued until the sinus has closed.
packs.text:
Because cellulitis is usually

present, the extremity is immobilized in a posterior splint,
and appropriate antibiotics are given. If gauze has been
used to pack the bursa, it is changed at least twice
weekly. Despite sufficient drainage, sinuses often persist
for a time on one or both sides of the knee. Immobiliza-
tion is continued until the sinus has closed.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — EXCISION OF BURSA
packs.summary: The patient should be informed when first seen that complete excision of the bursa may be necessary if healing fails to occur after simple drainage. If the walls of the bursa are thickened from chronic inflammation, resecting the entire bursa usually is easy, but if the lesion is acute and the effusion is serous, excising the bursa completely may be impossible; however, enough can be excised to relieve symptoms.
packs.text:
The patient should be informed when first seen that complete excision of the bursa may be necessary if healing fails to occur after simple drainage. If the walls of the bursa are thickened from chronic inflammation, resecting the entire bursa usually is easy, but if the lesion is acute and the effusion is serous, excising the bursa completely may be impossible;
however, enough can be excised to relieve symptoms.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 10.6
packs.summary: 1. Make a longitudinal incision of appropriate length centered over the bursa. 2. Dissect the bursal sac from the overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue and from the patellar aponeurosis beneath it. 3. If possible, excise the bursa without rupturing or perforating it. 4. Trim away the redundant skin, obtain complete hemo- stasis, and close the wound primarily. 5. Because the most common complication after excising a superficial bursa is a large hematoma, obliteration of the dead space by inserting one or more mattress sutures through the skin and deeper tissues on each side of the incision is recommended. The skin is then opposed with interrupted sutures. In an attempt to close down dead space, mattress sutures can be tied over large buttons.
packs.text:
1. Make a longitudinal incision of appropriate length centered over the bursa.

2. Dissect the bursal sac from the overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue and from the patellar aponeurosis beneath it.

3. If possible, excise the bursa without rupturing or perforating it.

4. Trim away the redundant skin, obtain complete hemo-
stasis, and close the wound primarily.

5. Because the most common complication after excising a superficial bursa is a large hematoma, obliteration of the dead space by inserting one or more mattress sutures through the skin and deeper tissues on each side of the incision is recommended. The skin is then opposed with interrupted sutures. In an attempt to close down dead space, mattress sutures can be tied over large buttons.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — POSTOPERATIVE CARE
packs.summary: A moderately large compres- sion dressing is applied, and the extremity is immobilized from groin to ankle for at least 2 weeks until the wound has healed completely. Alternatively, suction drainage can be used to obliterate the dead space. Quadriceps-setting exercises are begun the day after surgery. Antibiotics are indicated if an infection is present or is possible.
packs.text:
A moderately large compres-

sion dressing is applied, and the extremity is immobilized
from groin to ankle for at least 2 weeks until the wound
has healed completely. Alternatively, suction drainage can
be used to obliterate the dead space. Quadriceps-setting
exercises are begun the day after surgery. Antibiotics are
indicated if an infection is present or is possible.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ENDOSCOPIC EXCISION OF SEPTIC PREPATELLAR BURSAE
packs.summary: Good or excellent results have been reported with endoscopic excision of septic prepatellar bursae performed through two or three small portals after failure of nonoperative treatment. No recurrences were reported.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TIBIAL COLLATERAL LIGAMENT FIBROSITIS AND BURSITIS
packs.summary: Bursae have been found between the longitudinal part of the tibial collateral ligament and the capsule of the knee (Fig. 10.7); these bursae may be located in five different posi- tions, and three have been found beneath the ligament in a single knee. Calcification in one or more of these bursae has been shown similar to Pellegrini–Stieda disease. We consider most disorders that cause pain and tenderness beneath the tibial collateral ligament (not directly opposite the knee joint) to be fibrositis of the ligament; most respond favorably to the injection of an appropriate steroid preparation and to other nonoperative measures. Tibial collateral ligament bursitis should be included in the differential diagnoses in patients with medial joint line pain and no history of mechanical symptoms...
packs.text:
Bursae have been found between the longitudinal part of
the tibial collateral ligament and the capsule of the knee
(Fig. 10.7); these bursae may be located in five different posi-
tions, and three have been found beneath the ligament in a
single knee. Calcification in one or more of these bursae has
been shown similar to Pellegrini–Stieda disease. We consider
most disorders that cause pain and tenderness beneath the
tibial collateral ligament (not directly opposite the knee joint)
to be fibrositis of the ligament; most respond favorably to the
injection of an appropriate steroid preparation and to other
nonoperative measures.

Tibial collateral ligament bursitis should be included in
the differential diagnoses in patients with medial joint line
pain and no history of mechanical symptoms of instability or
laxity. Tenderness usually is localized to just below the joint
line. This can be treated with local steroid injection followed
by early exercise. If symptoms do not respond to one or two
injections, MRI or arthroscopy should be considered for
evaluation of possible intraarticular derangement or stress
fracture of the medial tibial plateau. Tibial collateral ligament
bursitis has a characteristic MRI appearance of fluid deep to
the tibial collateral ligament in the shape of an inverted U. We
have seen several patients who were unresponsive to one or

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — Fibular collateral ligament bursitis
packs.summary: Bursitis beneath the fibular collateral ligament causes localized, tender swelling on the lateral side of the knee and often is con- fused with a cyst of the lateral meniscus. The distended bursa varies from 0.6 to 2.5 cm or more in diameter, is extrasynovial, and lies beneath or just anterior or posterior to the fibular col- lateral ligament. Varus strain of the knee is painful, but typical signs and symptoms of an internal derangement of the knee are absent. Other lesions that should be considered include biceps tendinitis, partial biceps avulsion with pain and popping at 30 to 45 degrees of flexion, and posterolateral popping caused by a previous injury to the posterolateral corner or by a bony tubercle that causes popping of the popliteus tendon. If a mass is not evident,...
packs.text:
Bursitis beneath the fibular collateral ligament causes localized,
tender swelling on the lateral side of the knee and often is con-
fused with a cyst of the lateral meniscus. The distended bursa
varies from 0.6 to 2.5 cm or more in diameter, is extrasynovial,
and lies beneath or just anterior or posterior to the fibular col-
lateral ligament. Varus strain of the knee is painful, but typical
signs and symptoms of an internal derangement of the knee are
absent. Other lesions that should be considered include biceps
tendinitis, partial biceps avulsion with pain and popping at
30 to 45 degrees of flexion, and posterolateral popping caused
by a previous injury to the posterolateral corner or by a bony
tubercle that causes popping of the popliteus tendon.

If a mass is not evident, injections of a local anesthetic
agent or a steroid preparation into the area of tenderness,
together with support and rest, usually relieve the symptoms.
When a mass is palpable, excision is curative.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — INFRAPATELLAR BURSITIS
packs.summary: A small, deep, subpatellar or infrapatellar bursa is located be- tween the tuberosity of the tibia and the patellar tendon and is separated from the synovium of the knee by a pad of fat. When distended, this bursa causes a fluctuant swelling that obliterates the depression on each side of the ligament. In- frapatellar bursal infection should be considered when symp- toms resemble septic arthritis or osteomyelitis of the proximal tibia, such as loss of full extension of the knee, resistance to full flexion, and maximal tenderness near the patellar liga- ment. The infrapatellar bursa should be aspirated, taking care not to enter the knee joint. If infection is found, immediate drainage is recommended in addition to evaluation of the proximal tibial metaphysis for evidence of...
packs.text:
A small, deep, subpatellar or infrapatellar bursa is located be-
tween the tuberosity of the tibia and the patellar tendon and is
separated from the synovium of the knee by a pad of fat.
When distended, this bursa causes a fluctuant swelling that
obliterates the depression on each side of the ligament. In-
frapatellar bursal infection should be considered when symp-
toms resemble septic arthritis or osteomyelitis of the proximal
tibia, such as loss of full extension of the knee, resistance to
full flexion, and maximal tenderness near the patellar liga-
ment. The infrapatellar bursa should be aspirated, taking care

not to enter the knee joint. If infection is found, immediate
drainage is recommended in addition to evaluation of the
proximal tibial metaphysis for evidence of osteomyelitis. A
sterile effusion of the knee joint, which may accompany an
infrapatellar bursitis, should not be confused with infection.
The bursa can be drained through a small medial parapatellar
incision without entering the knee joint. A knee immobilizer
is used after surgery until acute symptoms have resolved, and
then range-of-motion exercises are begun.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — POPLITEAL CYST (BAKER CYST)
packs.summary: A Baker cyst, described by Baker in 1877, has since borne his name even though it was described previously by Adams in 1840. In most instances, a Baker, or popliteal, cyst is a dis- tended bursa. Numerous bursae are located in the popliteal space between the hamstring tendons and the collateral liga- ments or condyles of the tibia; a bursa also is located deep to each head of the gastrocnemius muscle. Symptoms develop most often in the bursa beneath the medial head of the gas- trocnemius or in the semimembranosus bursa; the latter is a double bursa located between the semimembranosus tendon and the medial tibial condyle and between the semi- membranosus tendon and the medial head of the gastrocne- mius. A popliteal cyst can be produced by herniation of the synovial membrane through the...
packs.text:
A Baker cyst, described by Baker in 1877, has since borne his name even though it was described previously by Adams in 1840. In most instances, a Baker, or popliteal, cyst is a dis-
tended bursa. Numerous bursae are located in the popliteal
space between the hamstring tendons and the collateral liga-
ments or condyles of the tibia; a bursa also is located deep to
each head of the gastrocnemius muscle. Symptoms develop
most often in the bursa beneath the medial head of the gas-
trocnemius or in the semimembranosus bursa; the latter
is a double bursa located between the semimembranosus
tendon and the medial tibial condyle and between the semi-
membranosus tendon and the medial head of the gastrocne-
mius. A popliteal cyst can be produced by herniation of the
synovial membrane through the posterior part of the capsule
of the knee or by the escape of fluid through the normal com-
munication of a bursa with the knee, that is, the semimem-
branosus or the medial gastrocnemius bursa. An association
has been found between the presence of capsular folds and
holes in the capsule and popliteal cysts. Development of acute
compartment syndrome from a ruptured Baker cyst and
spontaneous venous bleeding have been reported. We have

Venous thrombosis should be excluded as part of the evaluation of suspected pseudothrombophlebitis caused by a dissecting or ruptured popliteal cyst.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — ■ DIAGNOSIS
packs.summary: Diagnosing a popliteal cyst usually is not difficult. One-third to one-half of patients with these cysts are children. The cyst must be distinguished from a lipoma, xanthoma, vascular tumor, fibrosarcoma, and other tumors; occasionally, the cyst may be confused with an aneurysm. A pyogenic abscess may sometimes be located in the popliteal space, but this can be diagnosed easily. Usually, the diagnosis can be made by trans- illuminating the cyst. Other diagnostic techniques, such as arthrography, MRI, and ultrasound, can be helpful in estab- lishing the diagnosis. MRI is the preferred modality because it also can show intraarticular pathology. In children, the cyst infrequently communicates with the joint, and intraarticular pathologic findings are rare. Rarely, a popliteal cyst can...
packs.text:
Diagnosing a popliteal cyst usually is not difficult. One-third to one-half of patients with these cysts are children. The cyst must be distinguished from a lipoma, xanthoma, vascular tumor, fibrosarcoma, and other tumors; occasionally, the cyst may be confused with an aneurysm. A pyogenic abscess may sometimes be located in the popliteal space, but this can be diagnosed easily. Usually, the diagnosis can be made by trans-
illuminating the cyst. Other diagnostic techniques, such as arthrography, MRI, and ultrasound, can be helpful in estab-
lishing the diagnosis. MRI is the preferred modality because it also can show intraarticular pathology. In children, the cyst infrequently communicates with the joint, and intraarticular pathologic findings are rare. Rarely, a popliteal cyst can dis-
sect down into the calf in an intramuscular path.

Giant synovial cysts of the calf often are associated with
rheumatoid arthritis. They arise from and communicate with
the knee in the popliteal area, as can be shown by arthrogra-
phy or MRI. If a popliteal cyst is suspected, arthrography or
MRI of the knee or ultrasound examination of the calf should
be done before excision.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TREATMENT
packs.summary: Treatment of a Baker cyst is usually conservative. As Baker cysts are often secondary problems, the most common surgical treatment of a Baker cyst is arthroscopic treatment of intraar- ticular pathology that is contributing to the excessive synovial fluid production. In our experience with a large series of chil- dren, these cysts generally resolve with benign neglect. Occa- sionally, aspiration may be attempted, provided that the diag- nosis is certain, and these aspirations are typically performed with ultrasound guidance. In adults, intraarticular pathologic findings are common, and the cyst can recur if the intraarticu- lar pathologic condition is not corrected. Intraarticular patho- logic conditions, such as patellofemoral chondromalacia or a degenerative tear of the posterior...
packs.text:
Treatment of a Baker cyst is usually conservative. As Baker cysts are often secondary problems, the most common surgical treatment of a Baker cyst is arthroscopic treatment of intraar-
ticular pathology that is contributing to the excessive synovial fluid production. In our experience with a large series of chil-
dren, these cysts generally resolve with benign neglect. Occa-
sionally, aspiration may be attempted, provided that the diag-
nosis is certain, and these aspirations are typically performed
with ultrasound guidance. In adults, intraarticular pathologic
findings are common, and the cyst can recur if the intraarticu-
lar pathologic condition is not corrected. Intraarticular patho-
logic conditions, such as patellofemoral chondromalacia or a
degenerative tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus,
can be identified and treated by debridement of loose carti-
laginous fragments or partial meniscectomy. Often, the associ-
ated pathology is a degenerative tear of the posterior horn of
the medial meniscus, and sometimes the cyst can be treated by
accessing arthroscopically through the meniscal defect to
widen the aperture and prevent accumulation of fluid in the
cyst. Arthroscopic excision of the cyst wall, enlarging the com-
munication between the articular cavity and the cyst, and ar-
throscopic management of intraarticular lesions have been
shown to produce the best

packs.summary: results. NOTE! Loose bodies can in- termittently travel between the intraarticular space and an ex- traarticular popliteal cyst. In almost all cases, the loose bodies will remain in the cyst; however, if desired, a capsular opening into the cyst can be performed, and the loose body can then be removed through an accessory posteromedial portal.

termittently travel between the intraarticular space and an ex-
traarticular popliteal cyst. In almost all cases, the loose bodies
will remain in the cyst; however, if desired, a capsular opening
into the cyst can be performed, and the loose body can then be

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TREATMENT
packs.summary: In patients with rheumatoid arthritis who have a giant syno vial cyst removed, a synovectomy should be performed later to prevent recurrence of the cyst (recurrence of <2% has been noted with posterior open cystectomy with valve and posterior capsule repair and arthroscopic treatment of intraarticular lesions). Significantly better outcomes have been reported after arthroscopic cystectomy compared with arthroscopy followed by open cystectomy or open cystectomy alone. A recurrence rate of 40% has been noted after open cystectomy alone. For arthroscopic cystectomy, posterior portals have been recom- mended for resection of the popliteal cyst origin. In addition, the capsular fold of the valvular mechanism of the popliteal cyst should be removed arthroscopically with a motorized shaver....
packs.text:
In patients with rheumatoid arthritis who have a giant syno
vial cyst removed, a synovectomy should be performed later
to prevent recurrence of the cyst (recurrence of <2% has been
noted with posterior open cystectomy with valve and posterior
capsule repair and arthroscopic treatment of intraarticular

lesions). Significantly better outcomes have been reported after arthroscopic cystectomy compared with arthroscopy followed by open cystectomy or open cystectomy alone. A recurrence rate of 40% has been noted after open cystectomy alone. For arthroscopic cystectomy, posterior portals have been recom-
mended for resection of the popliteal cyst origin. In addition,
the capsular fold of the valvular mechanism of the popliteal cyst
should be removed arthroscopically with a motorized shaver.

If the cyst does not appear to communicate with the joint or
if significant changes cannot be treated arthroscopically, an open
procedure can be performed. Most cysts can be approached by a
posteromedial (Henderson) incision. Very large or midline
lesions can be approached through a posterior incision.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — POPLITEAL CYST EXCISION
packs.summary: A popliteal cyst can be excised posteromedially through a medial hockey-stick incision. The procedure can be performed with the patient supine. If an arthroscopic evalu- ation is part of the procedure, the leg does not have to be rescrubbed or redraped, and the patient does not need to be turned prone for open excision of the cyst.
packs.text:
A popliteal cyst can be excised posteromedially through a medial hockey-stick incision. The procedure can be performed with the patient supine. If an arthroscopic evalu-
ation is part of the procedure, the leg does not have to be rescrubbed or redraped, and the patient does not need to
be turned prone for open excision of the cyst.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (HUGHSTON, BAKER, AND MELLO)
packs.summary: 1. With the patient supine, externally rotate the hip fully and flex the knee to 90 degrees. Make a medial hockey- stick incision at the joint line (Fig. 10.8A). 2. Use only the posteromedial portion of the incision if an arthroscopic examination already has been performed. Otherwise, inspect the joint through an anteromedial retinacular incision (Fig. 10.8B). 3. Make a posteromedial capsular incision beginning be- tween the medial epicondyle and adductor tubercle along the posterior border of the tibial collateral liga- ment (Fig. 10.8B). 4. Retract the posterior oblique ligament posteriorly and inspect the posteromedial compartment. Identify the popliteal cyst; it is usually in the area between the me- dial head of the gastrocnemius and semimembranosus tendon (Fig. 10.8B). 5. Inspect...
packs.text:
1. With the patient supine, externally rotate the hip fully
and flex the knee to 90 degrees. Make a medial hockey-
stick incision at the joint line (Fig. 10.8A).

2. Use only the posteromedial portion of the incision if an arthroscopic examination already has been performed.
Otherwise, inspect the joint through an anteromedial retinacular incision (Fig. 10.8B).

3. Make a posteromedial capsular incision beginning be-
tween the medial epicondyle and adductor tubercle
along the posterior border of the tibial collateral liga-
ment (Fig. 10.8B).

4. Retract the posterior oblique ligament posteriorly and inspect the posteromedial compartment. Identify the popliteal cyst; it is usually in the area between the me-
dial head of the gastrocnemius and semimembranosus tendon (Fig. 10.8B).

5. Inspect the posteromedial joint and cyst lining for an
intraarticular communication (Fig. 10.8C).

6. Separate the adherent cyst lining from the surrounding soft tissues and trace it to the posterior capsule.

7. Excise the cyst at the base of its stalk on the capsule.
8. Close the orifice, if possible, with one or two nonab-
sorbable sutures.

metadata.source_book: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026)

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — (HUGHSTON, BAKER, AND MELLO)
packs.summary: 9. At closure, the posterior oblique ligament may be lax because of pressure from the cyst beneath it. If it is lax, advance it onto the medial epicondyle and tibial collat- eral ligament to restore tension to the posteromedial capsular ligaments and semimembranosus capsular aponeurosis. Close the wound in layers. POSTOPERATIVE CARE The limb is placed in a knee immobilizer, and weight bearing to tolerance is allowed. Straight leg raising and quadriceps-setting exercises are begun on the first day after surgery. The immobilizer is
packs.text:
9. At closure, the posterior oblique ligament may be lax
because of pressure from the cyst beneath it. If it is lax,
advance it onto the medial epicondyle and tibial collat-
eral ligament to restore tension to the posteromedial
capsular ligaments and semimembranosus capsular
aponeurosis. Close the wound in layers.

POSTOPERATIVE CARE The limb is placed in a knee immobilizer, and weight bearing to tolerance is allowed.
Straight leg raising and quadriceps-setting exercises are begun on the first day after surgery. The immobilizer is

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — MEDIAL GASTROCNEMIUS BURSITIS
packs.summary: If the medial gastrocnemius bursa is involved, a palpable mass is located in the midline of the popliteal space or ex- tends beneath the head of the gastrocnemius and manifests between the medial head of the muscle and the semimembra- nosus tendon, simulating an enlarged semimembranosus bursa. In the latter instance, the bursa is excised through a posteromedial incision after arthroscopy with the patient supine, as described for semimembranosus bursitis; when in the midline of the popliteal space, it is excised as follows.

packs.text:
FIGURE 10.9 Meyerding and Van Demark removal of mid-line Baker cyst. A, Skin incision. B, After being exposed, pedicle is clamped, ligated, divided, and inverted. SEE TECHNIQUE 10.8.

2. Divide the deep fascia, expose the protruding sac, and by blunt dissection free it down to its attachment to the posterior aspect of the capsule of the knee. In some instances, relaxing the muscles and tendons on each side of the cyst by flexing the knee increases the exposure.

3. Clamp the pedicle of the cyst at its attachment to the capsule of the joint and divide it, but leave enough pedicle to permit its inversion (Fig. 10.9B).
4. Invert the pedicle and close it; Meyering and Van Demark recommended permanent sutures for this closure.

POSTOPERATIVE CARE The postoperative care is the
same as for Technique 10.7.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — SEMIMEMBRANOSUS BURSITIS
packs.summary: A distended semimembranosus bursa occurs on the medial side of the popliteal space on the medial or more usually the lateral side of the semimembranosus tendon. The bursa can be approached easily through a posteromedial incision with the patient supine or through a posterior incision as described.
packs.text:
A distended semimembranosus bursa occurs on the medial
side of the popliteal space on the medial or more usually the
lateral side of the semimembranosus tendon. The bursa
can be approached easily through a posteromedial incision
with the patient supine or through a posterior incision as
described.

packs.title: Campbell's Operative Orthopaedics, 15th Ed (2026) — TECHNIQUE 10.9
packs.summary: 1. With the patient supine, make a slightly oblique or curved longitudinal incision 7.5 cm long over the medial aspect of the popliteal space; a common error is to place this incision too far proximally. Incise the deep fascia; the proper plane for dissection is then usually evident. 2. Develop the interval between the semimembranosus and the medial head of the gastrocnemius and separate the cyst wall from these structures. No important nerves or vessels lie in this plane of cleavage. 3. Continue the dissection; it becomes increasingly difficult to separate the cyst from adjacent structures. In the depths of the wound, the wall usually is adherent and requires sharp dissection that should include some of the fibrous parts of the semimembranosus or of the gastrocnemius; otherwise, the...
packs.text:
1. With the patient supine, make a slightly oblique or curved longitudinal incision 7.5 cm long over the medial aspect of the popliteal space; a common error is to place this incision too far proximally. Incise the deep fascia; the proper plane for dissection is then usually evident.
2. Develop the interval between the semimembranosus and the medial head of the gastrocnemius and separate the cyst wall from these structures. No important nerves or vessels lie in this plane of cleavage.
3. Continue the dissection; it becomes increasingly difficult to separate the cyst from adjacent structures. In the depths of the wound, the wall usually is adherent and requires sharp dissection that should include some of the fibrous parts of the semimembranosus or of the gastrocnemius; otherwise, the cyst is ruptured, making it difficult to outline and to determine whether it has a pedicle or communicates with the joint.
4. Often the base of the cyst is intimately attached to the capsule and synovium. A small opening may be located, if desired, by injecting air or a physiologic saline solution containing a little methylene blue through the posterior part of the capsule into the joint, but closing any opening in the joint capsule probably is unnecessary.
5. The technique for excising cysts on the medial side of the semimembranosus tendon is performed in a similar manner.

packs.summary: POSTOPERATIVE CARE Postoperative care is the same as for Technique 10.7.
packs.text:
